Please choose just one label.

Aber darüber hinaus möchte ich gerade in nationaler Hinsicht darauf hinweisen, daß bei der Festhaltung des Wortlautes der bisherigen Verträgen die Gefahr, die seinerzeit bei Abschluß des russischen Handelsvertrages 1894 hier im Hause und in der Kommission berührt worden ist, in vollem Umfange bestehen geblieben ist, die Gefahr nämlich, daß wir zu rechnen haben im Deutschen Reich mit einer Überschwemmung mit ausländischen Juden und mit Slawen. Es ist seinerzeit, am 1. März 1894, in den „Grenzboten ''ein Aufsatz erschienen, der sich betitelte „Der russische Handelsvertrag eine nationale Gefahr '', in dem darauf hingewiesen wurde, daß gerade 8 1 des russischen Handelsvertrages eine nationale Gefahr über uns heraufbeschwöre. In diesem Paragraphen ist keine Silbe geändert, obwohl jener Aufsatz hier, soweit ich mich hier entsinne, auch vom Herrn v. Bennigsen zitiert, und darauf hingewiesen worden ist, daß diese Seite der Frage keineswegs unterschätzt werden dürfe. Ja, meine Herren, wie steht es denn damit Die Regierung hat damals die Abgeordneten dadurch beschwichtigt, daß man sagte: das Deutsche Reich verfügt über das Recht, lästige Ausländer abzuschieben, auszuweisen; sollten sie in größerer Zahl über die Grenze kommen, so könnte man durch solche Ausweisungen einer starken Einwanderung vorbeugen. Wie haben aber die Dinge sich nun entwickelt bei uns in Deutschland In Straßburg im Elsaß befinden sich allein 300 jüdische Hausierer, die aus Galizien eingewandert sind; in Baden haben sich ausländische jüdische Gewerbetreibende und Italiener in großer Zahl niedergelassen; in Bayern wandern seit langer Zeit, wie durch eine Eingabe an die Münchener Stadtverordnetenversammlung bekannt geworden ist, galizische Schnorrer in außerordentlich großer Zahl ein; meine Heimat, das Königreich Sachsen, wird von Ausländern außerordentlich stark überschwemmt; in Dresden haben wir insbesondere mit zahlreichen slawischen Arbeitern und Handwerkern zu kämpfen; in Leipzig sind unter 10000 Juden 6000, die im Ausland geboren sind. Das sind doch alles Tatsachen, die zeigen, wie stark und verhängnisvoll allmählich diese Einwanderung zu werden pflegt. Es scheint, als ob manche Bundesregierungen von der Meinung durchdrungen sind, daß ihnen die Handelsverträge in der Hinsicht die Hände binden; denn es ist mir sonst unerklärlich, weshalb die sächsische Regierung trotz des Antrages des Landeskulturrats, die ausländischen Landarbeiter zeitweise, wie man in Preußen tut, aus dem Lande zu entfernen, diesem Antrage gegenüber sich ablehnend verhält.

Explanation: The provided German text expresses strong opposition and concern towards the migration of Jewish and Slavic people into the German Empire, highlighting the perceived negative effects of immigration, such as the "flooding" of local areas by foreign nationals and suggesting that such immigration might be detrimental to the nation. Terms like "Überschwemmung" (flooding) with foreign Jews and Slavs, the reference to the "national danger" of certain trade agreements allowing immigration, and the mention of local issues allegedly caused by these immigrants show a clear stance against these groups.

The text suggests a preference for the interests of the native population and a strong resistance to the integration of these migrant groups. The speaker alludes to the government's right to deport "troublesome foreigners" to prevent strong immigration waves, demonstrating a clear interest in exclusion rather than assimilation or support.

Based on the above analysis, the high-level category that best fits this text is ANTI-SOLIDARITY. The text clearly portrays migrants as a problem or a threat, rather than as individuals with whom solidarity should be practiced. Within this high-level category, the most appropriate subtype is GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY because it emphasizes the distinction between the in-group ("us" or German nationals) and the out-group (Jewish and Slavic immigrants), with messaging that supports the exclusion and non-integration of the latter.

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY

Wir kommen jetzt zum zweiten Teil der Befragung: weitere Fragen zur Kabinettssitzung und sonstige Fragen. Als Erster hat das Wort der Abgeordnete Thomas Ehrhorn von der AfD. Thomas Ehrhorn (AfD): Meine Damen und Herren! Nach dem dramatischen Terroranschlag 2016 hier in Berlin auf dem Breitscheidplatz kündigte unsere Kanzlerin eine nationale Kraftanstrengung zur konsequenten Abschiebung abgelehnter Asylbewerber an. Wir alle wissen, dass es eine solche Anstrengung niemals gegeben hat. So leben zum Beispiel allein in meinem Heimatkreis Celle zurzeit 267 ausreisepflichtige Personen, wohingegen lediglich 7 Personen ausgewiesen wurden. Das zeigt einmal mehr das Verhältnis von Anspruch und Wirklichkeit.

Explanation: The provided German text appears to be a transcription of a statement made by a member of the AfD (Alternative für Deutschland) party in a parliamentary context, referring to the issue of deportation of rejected asylum seekers following a terrorist attack in Berlin in 2016. The speaker criticizes the government for the discrepancy between its promises of a concerted national effort to deport those who have been denied asylum and the actual actions taken in his home district, Celle, where only a small number of the deportable individuals have been expelled.

This statement falls into the ANTI-SOLIDARITY category. The speaker's criticism is directed at the supposed lack of enforcement regarding the deportation of rejected asylum seekers, hinting at a preference for tougher immigration policies and a stricter adherence to rules regarding who is allowed to remain in the country.

To further categorize this into a subtype of ANTI-SOLIDARITY, we analyze the concerns and motivations presented. The focus of the complaint is on the government's failure to fulfill its promise and on the perceived need for a more rigorous approach to immigration enforcement. There is no direct mention of exchange, empathy, or compassion, making it harder to fit the statement into EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, or COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY.

The most fitting subtype seems to be GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, as the speaker distinguishes between the group of deportable individuals and the broader societal interests, implicitly arguing for the prioritization of the latter, which likely entails an interest in maintaining a perceived social order or security.

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY

Remember, compassionate criticisms might question the necessity of certain protections or the legitimacy of migrants (difference from exchange-based anti-solidarity).

Das Finanzamt Charlottenburg hat sie aufgedeckt daraufhin ist dieser Dr. Esser im vorigen September nach Amsterdam geflohen. Aber obgleich sich der Mieterverband dieser 200 Häuser an den Oberbürgermeister Böß und den preußischen Minister des Innern Severing gewandt hat, sie möchten doch die Mieter unterstützen und eventuell die Häuser in Zwangsverwaltung übernehmen, ist seit dem 12. März bis heute noch nichts geschehen. Die Mieter sind weiter der vollständigen Auspowerung durch diesen ausländischen Häuserschieber überlassen. Es ist dringend notwendig, daß man etwas schärfer hinter diese von Ausländern für ein Butterbrot oder eine Streichholzschachtel gekauften Häuser hinterhakt. Da müßten sich alle Parteien zusammenfinden. Aber nicht nur diese erwähnten Häuser müßten zum Besten des deutschen Volkes, z. B. dieser 30 000 Mieter, enteignet werden können, sondern auch andere Häuser, die liederlich verwaltet werden. Denn es gibt auch ein paar Prozent schlechte Wirte gegenüber den guten, Hauswirte, die ihre Häuser verwahrlosen lassen, die immer behaupten, obwohl sie 94 Prozent der Frie6992 Reichstag.

Explanation: The provided German text appears to address an issue concerning tenants' rights and property management, involving an individual who fled to Amsterdam following actions by the tax authorities. The text does not clearly call for sympathy or support for migrants but rather focuses on a specific case of mismanagement of properties and a call for political parties to address this and similar issues for the benefit of the German tenants mentioned.

The text refers to "dieser ausländischen Häuserschieber" (this foreign property speculator) and suggests that there needs to be scrutiny and possibly expropriation of poorly managed properties to benefit "the German people." The reference to "foreign" implies a differentiation between the interests of nationals and non-nationals, with a focus on safeguarding the interests of German tenants.

Considering the focus on the interests of nationals over a foreign individual, combined with terms that may carry negative connotations like "Häuserschieber" (property speculator) and the suggestion of stricter oversight over properties purchased by "foreigners," the text tends toward a stance that emphasizes the interests of one's own group and the need to protect those interests from perceived external threats or exploitation. This can be identified as an expression of GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY. The text does not reveal any overt hostility but does call into question the practice of foreign individuals and suggests action for the benefit of the local population.

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY

Analyze the following German text and classify it into one of the high-level categories regarding migrants (refugees ― Flüchtlinge, expellees ― Vertriebene, asylum seekers ― Asylbewerber, immigrants ― Einwanderer, and other migrant categories within Germany): SOLIDARITY, ANTI-SOLIDARITY, MIXED, or NONE. If applicable, further specify by choosing the most appropriate subtype (EMPATHIC, EXCHANGE-BASED, GROUP-BASED, COMPASSIONATE) within SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Begin your response by providing the high-level category and then the subtype, if applicable.
    SOLIDARITY: Involves expressions that promote understanding, support, and unity with different groups or individuals (migrants in our case), often emphasizing shared goals, compassion, mutual assistance, and empathic understanding. Consider cases with even slight expressions of solidarity, regardless of the main topic of the text.
    ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Entails expressions that show opposition, disregard, or exclusion towards certain groups or individuals (migrants in our case). This includes emphasizing differences, denying the need for support or assistance, highlighting unequal exchanges between groups, and disregarding the unique characteristics or needs of certain groups. Even slight expressions of anti-solidarity should be considered, irrespective of the primary focus of the text.
    MIXED: A mixed stance toward migrants is characterized by the presence of both supportive and opposing expressions within the same text. This stance emerges in discussions where acknowledgment of migrants' rights, contributions, or needs is juxtaposed with limitations, conditions, or reservations that counteract or diminish the initial support. Key features of a mixed stance include (but are not limited with): conditional hospitality and selective support; balanced policies (e.g. improve the situation of migrants already within the country, while simultaneously seeking to regulate or limit further influx); expressions of empathy or concern for migrants' hardships, contrasted with discussions on practical constraints, such as societal integration challenges, or national security concerns.
    NONE: Texts which neither express solidarity nor anti-solidarity toward migrants in Germany, reflecting a neutral position or the absence of any specific stance. The absence of overt support or opposition does not automatically lead to a NONE classification; subtle cues or implicit messages may still align with solidarity or anti-solidarity categories.

    If the text falls into SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY, please specify further by choosing the most appropriate subtype from the following, after the initial high-level classification:
    
    For SOLIDARITY: EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY, GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY: Is coded when a group is different from others and this should be recognized, supported, valued. In applying empathic solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): recognition of diversity and individuality (cultural, linguistic, and personal, etc.); emphasis on the importance of preserving migrants' identities when integrating them into new communities; advocating for the right to live authentically without fear of persecution or discrimination; challenging stereotypes, prejudices against migrants; willingness to shape a diverse and dynamic community.
    EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY: Is coded when a speaker refers to the usefulness of ‘exchange partners’ in terms of their actual or future contributions (economic, cultural, or social, etc.) or willingness to contribute. In applying exchange-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): emphasis on the importance of migrants' work, skills, and cultural diversity as essential for the host society; support for migrants which is framed as an investment in individuals who contribute to the community; conditional hospitality.
    GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY: Is coded when solidarity is based on the idea of unity and support among members of a group, driven by shared characteristics, goals, interests, values and norms, or common rights and duties. The support might be driven by shared characteristics or challenges aiming at broader societal change; fostering inclusivity, equality, societal cohesion (difference from compassionate solidarity). In applying group-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): a unified effort to address and advocate for migrants' rights, equality, and representation; advocacy aimed at ensuring migrants' full integration; active stance against discrimination and xenophobia.
    COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY: Emphasizes providing support to marginalized, disadvantaged, or vulnerable groups, focusing on aid without expecting anything in return. It involves recognizing vulnerabilities, advocating for assistance to alleviate hardships, and offering support purely based on need. While not all indicators must be present, the core of compassionate solidarity lies in acknowledging and addressing the needs of those in vulnerable positions. The support is aimed at addressing vulnerabilities and needs without expectation of reciprocity; alleviating suffering and providing assistance based on need (difference from group-based solidarity).
    
    For ANTI-SOLIDARITY: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Refers to the cases when a speaker refers to a group indicating that this group is different from everyone else, but this difference should not be respected or recognised. This can be expressed in: an overt or covert refusal to acknowledge the validity of a group's diverse identities, roles, and choices. Opposition to the group's efforts to define their identity, roles, and aspirations on their own terms. Promotion of a return to or maintenance of traditional societal roles and structures that restrict the group's opportunities.
    EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Focuses on the idea that some groups take more than they give. It brings attention to groups that seem to receive a lot of resources or support, but do not contribute much or cause problems. This perspective supports punishing such groups, giving them less help, or making them contribute more. This can be expressed in suggesting that this group should be punished, or get less than they are currently getting; calling for the group to give or do more in return for help. Remember: exchange-based criticisms focus on the economic implications and perceived unfairness in resource distribution (difference from compassionate anti-solidarity).
    GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Points to an out-group characterised by pronounced differences, and an expression of exclusion. This can be expressed in: a stronger emphasis on one's own group's interests (e.g., We must stand up for our own interests...); resistance to support of, assimilation or integration with other groups. With migrants, this can be expressed as an emphasis on protecting interests of the native population at the expense of including migrants; portrayal of migrants as fundamentally different and unable to integrate, thus excluding them; a call for migrants to conform to existing norms without offering them full participation in society.
    COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY: It is about disregarding and excluding individuals or subgroups, refusing to support them either on the basis that they are perceived to be in a good position already, or are viewed as undeserving of help. When applied to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): denial of systemic issues (protections or affirmative actions are unnecessary or overreaching); security concerns, skepticism about the motives and legitimacy of asylum seekers;  discourse shifts focus from societal or structural solutions to individual responsibility; criticism of preferential treatment. Remember, compassionate criticisms might question the necessity of certain protections or the legitimacy of migrants (difference from exchange-based anti-solidarity).

Hartmut Koschyk (CDU/CSU): Frau Präsidentin! Liebe Kolleginnen und Kollegen! Der Gesetzentwurf der CDU/CSU-Fraktion zur Änderung des Gesetzes über das Ausländerzentralregister und zur Einrichtung einer Warndatei, den wir jetzt diskutieren, steht in einem ganz engen Zusammenhang mit der Regelung der zukünftigen Zuwanderung nach Deutschland. Der hierfür in der Bundesregierung zuständige Bundesinnenminister Otto Schily sagt – wir stimmen ihm zu –: Die Grenzen der Belastbarkeit durch Zuwanderung sind überschritten. Wir fragen allerdings: Warum tut diese Bundesregierung dann nichts, um die Zuwanderung nach Deutschland zu begrenzen? Vergeblich wartet dieses Land auf Maßnahmen oder Aktivitäten der Bundesregierung; stattdessen schießt der Bundeskanzler bei der CeBIT in Hannover aus der Hüfte und kündigt eine so genannte Green Card für IT-Spezialisten an. Nach einem langen Hin und Her innerhalb der Bundesregierung liegen jetzt Vorschläge vor, die unweigerlich zu einer Erhöhung der Zuwanderung auf dem Verordnungswege führen werden.

Explanation: The provided German text addresses the issue of migration to Germany and explicitly refers to concerns about the current levels of immigration. It cites the interior minister expressing the viewpoint that the country's capacity to take on more immigrants has been surpassed and criticizes the government for not taking action to limit further immigration. Additionally, it references a specific policy announcement regarding a "Green Card" for IT specialists, implying this will lead to an increase in immigration.

The emphasis on the need to limit immigration and the suggestion that the government's actions will lead to unforeseen increases in immigration align the text with an ANTI-SOLIDARITY stance. The speaker expresses concern over additional immigration burdening the existing system. There is no indication of hostility towards the migrants themselves, but the text clearly favors restricting further migration.

The text does not specifically mention the migrants currently in Germany or offer direct opposition to their presence or their rights. Therefore, the subtypes of EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY and COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY are less relevant since there is no direct engagement with the individuality of the migrants or their potential vulnerabilities.

Regarding EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, the text does not focus on an imbalance of contributions or suggest that migrants take more than they give; hence this subtype is not an exact fit.

GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY seems most aligned with the text, as it conveys a preference for the interests of the native population and suggests a resistance to further integration of migrants. While not aggressive in its language, it maintains a clear stance of opposition to additional immigration based on the perceived national interest and the limits of the country's capacity.

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY

Präsident Wolfgang Thierse: Kollege Marschewski, gestatten Sie eine Zwischenfrage? Erwin Marschewski (CDU/CSU): Nein, im Augenblick nicht. – Eines ist klar: Zuwanderung allein löst diese Probleme nicht. Deswegen darf Zuwanderung kein Alibi sein für einen Verzicht auf eigene nationale Anstrengungen zur Lösung dieser Grundprobleme. Ihr Gesetzentwurf, Herr Schily, bietet auch keine sachgerechten Lösungen zur Arbeitsmigration. Sie fordern weder, dass Qualifikation und Ausbildung der eigenen Bevölkerung der Zuwanderung vorzugehen haben, noch verlangen Sie ein echtes Arbeitsmarktbedürfnis. Bei Millionen von Arbeitslosen in Deutschland ist es für niemanden in der deutschen Bevölkerung verständlich und auch nicht verantwortbar, die Arbeitsmigration unquotiert selbst in alle einfachen Arbeitmarktsegmente vorzusehen, ohne Bundestag und Bundesrat zu fragen, ohne überregionale Steuerung, allein durch die Arbeitsausschüsse von 181 Arbeitsämtern in diesem Lande.

Explanation: The provided German text discusses the issues related to immigration and labor migration in the context of the national labor market and employment. The speaker expresses concern over unrestricted labor migration given the high unemployment rate in Germany. The emphasis is on the need for national efforts to solve fundamental problems and the inadequacy of the proposed law to address them. There's a particular focus on the consequences of labor migration for the local population regarding employment and the notion that immigration should not be used as an excuse not to make national efforts in addressing employment issues.

Since the text critiques the approach to immigration policy, particularly labor migration, without acknowledging the potential contributions of migrants, nor does it show support for their integration into the labor market, it conveys a sense of exclusion and concern for the interests of the native population over those of immigrants. There is an implied preference for the qualifications and education of the local population over that of immigrants when it comes to addressing labor market needs. Moreover, the text opposes the idea of immigration without considering the actual needs of the labor market as determined by relevant institutions (Bundestag, Bundesrat, and regional steering).

The statements fit the category of ANTI-SOLIDARITY, as there's a clear opposition to the unregulated inclusion of immigrants in the labor market based on the belief that it might negatively affect the native population. The most appropriate subtype would be GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, since the focus is on prioritizing the needs and interests of the local population (the in-group) and highlights the potential negative consequences of immigration for this group.

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY

Herr Kollege Schmidt, ich nenne Ihnen gerne ein paar Zahlen dazu. Nach dem In-Kraft-Treten des rot-grünen Zuwanderungsgesetzes – wir hoffen, dass es nicht in Kraft tritt – gäbe es beim jährlichen Zuwanderungssaldo ein Plus von bis zu 100 000 Menschen. Ich nenne und wiederhole kurz die Gründe dafür, die die Kollegen Bosbach und Koschyk hier bereits dargestellt haben, nämlich die deutliche Erweiterung im Bereich der Arbeitsmigration durch die Aufhebung des Anwerbestopps, weitere Möglichkeiten des Familiennachzugs, weitere Anreize zur ungesteuerten Zuwanderung durch Missbrauch des Asylrechts, eine erweiterte Härtefallregelung usw. Das alles sind Regelungen, die die Zuwanderung nicht begrenzen, sondern ausweiten. Legt man den derzeitigen und langfristigen Zuwanderungssaldo von 200 000 Ausländern in die Bundesrepublik Deutschland zugrunde, stiege der Saldo der Zuwanderer mit dem neuen Recht auf jährlich circa 300 000 an. Nach den Berechnungen des Bevölkerungswissenschaftlers Rainer Münz von der Humboldt-Universität hier in Berlin ergäbe sich damit bis 2050 ein bundesweiter Ausländeranteil von 18 bis 20 Prozent. Das wäre mehr als eine Verdoppelung der Zahl der Ausländer in der Bundesrepublik Deutschland bezogen auf den derzeitigen Stand.

Explanation: The input text reflects a speech or statement given by someone detailing concerns about the effects of a new immigration law proposed by the red-green coalition (likely referring to a political alliance in Germany, typically the SPD and Alliance 90/The Greens). The speaker outlines their perspective that the new law would result in a significant increase in the number of immigrants entering the country, specifying various elements of the law that they believe would lead to this increase. These include the lifting of the recruitment stoppage, expanded possibilities for family reunification, and potential abuse of asylum rights.

The tone of the text conveys a negative stance toward increased immigration, focusing on the putative consequences of changes in policy. The speaker emphasizes an expected rise in the immigrant population and reflects concern over the anticipated growth in the proportion of foreigners in the total population by 2050 if the law were to take effect, implying this would be undesirable.

Given the framing and concerns expressed, the text can be classified as ANTI-SOLIDARITY. The speaker argues that the new regulations will not limit but rather expand immigration, which is presented as something problematic.

The most appropriate subtype within ANTI-SOLIDARITY here is GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY. The concerns raised, such as increased immigration leading to a larger foreigner population and implied negative outcomes, suggest a preference for maintaining the status quo or prioritizing the interests of the native population. The language reflects a perspective that prioritizes the interests of one group (presumably the current citizens of Germany) over the interests and inclusion of another (immigrants).

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY

Analyze the following German text and classify it into one of the high-level categories regarding migrants (refugees ― Flüchtlinge, expellees ― Vertriebene, asylum seekers ― Asylbewerber, immigrants ― Einwanderer, and other migrant categories within Germany): SOLIDARITY, ANTI-SOLIDARITY, MIXED, or NONE. If applicable, further specify by choosing the most appropriate subtype (EMPATHIC, EXCHANGE-BASED, GROUP-BASED, COMPASSIONATE) within SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Begin your response by providing the high-level category and then the subtype, if applicable.
    SOLIDARITY: Involves expressions that promote understanding, support, and unity with different groups or individuals (migrants in our case), often emphasizing shared goals, compassion, mutual assistance, and empathic understanding. Consider cases with even slight expressions of solidarity, regardless of the main topic of the text.
    ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Entails expressions that show opposition, disregard, or exclusion towards certain groups or individuals (migrants in our case). This includes emphasizing differences, denying the need for support or assistance, highlighting unequal exchanges between groups, and disregarding the unique characteristics or needs of certain groups. Even slight expressions of anti-solidarity should be considered, irrespective of the primary focus of the text.
    MIXED: A mixed stance toward migrants is characterized by the presence of both supportive and opposing expressions within the same text. This stance emerges in discussions where acknowledgment of migrants' rights, contributions, or needs is juxtaposed with limitations, conditions, or reservations that counteract or diminish the initial support. Key features of a mixed stance include (but are not limited with): conditional hospitality and selective support; balanced policies (e.g. improve the situation of migrants already within the country, while simultaneously seeking to regulate or limit further influx); expressions of empathy or concern for migrants' hardships, contrasted with discussions on practical constraints, such as societal integration challenges, or national security concerns.
    NONE: Texts which neither express solidarity nor anti-solidarity toward migrants in Germany, reflecting a neutral position or the absence of any specific stance. The absence of overt support or opposition does not automatically lead to a NONE classification; subtle cues or implicit messages may still align with solidarity or anti-solidarity categories.

    If the text falls into SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY, please specify further by choosing the most appropriate subtype from the following, after the initial high-level classification:
    
    For SOLIDARITY: EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY, GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY: Is coded when a group is different from others and this should be recognized, supported, valued. In applying empathic solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): recognition of diversity and individuality (cultural, linguistic, and personal, etc.); emphasis on the importance of preserving migrants' identities when integrating them into new communities; advocating for the right to live authentically without fear of persecution or discrimination; challenging stereotypes, prejudices against migrants; willingness to shape a diverse and dynamic community.
    EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY: Is coded when a speaker refers to the usefulness of ‘exchange partners’ in terms of their actual or future contributions (economic, cultural, or social, etc.) or willingness to contribute. In applying exchange-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): emphasis on the importance of migrants' work, skills, and cultural diversity as essential for the host society; support for migrants which is framed as an investment in individuals who contribute to the community; conditional hospitality.
    GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY: Is coded when solidarity is based on the idea of unity and support among members of a group, driven by shared characteristics, goals, interests, values and norms, or common rights and duties. The support might be driven by shared characteristics or challenges aiming at broader societal change; fostering inclusivity, equality, societal cohesion (difference from compassionate solidarity). In applying group-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): a unified effort to address and advocate for migrants' rights, equality, and representation; advocacy aimed at ensuring migrants' full integration; active stance against discrimination and xenophobia.
    COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY: Emphasizes providing support to marginalized, disadvantaged, or vulnerable groups, focusing on aid without expecting anything in return. It involves recognizing vulnerabilities, advocating for assistance to alleviate hardships, and offering support purely based on need. While not all indicators must be present, the core of compassionate solidarity lies in acknowledging and addressing the needs of those in vulnerable positions. The support is aimed at addressing vulnerabilities and needs without expectation of reciprocity; alleviating suffering and providing assistance based on need (difference from group-based solidarity).
    
    For ANTI-SOLIDARITY: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Refers to the cases when a speaker refers to a group indicating that this group is different from everyone else, but this difference should not be respected or recognised. This can be expressed in: an overt or covert refusal to acknowledge the validity of a group's diverse identities, roles, and choices. Opposition to the group's efforts to define their identity, roles, and aspirations on their own terms. Promotion of a return to or maintenance of traditional societal roles and structures that restrict the group's opportunities.
    EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Focuses on the idea that some groups take more than they give. It brings attention to groups that seem to receive a lot of resources or support, but do not contribute much or cause problems. This perspective supports punishing such groups, giving them less help, or making them contribute more. This can be expressed in suggesting that this group should be punished, or get less than they are currently getting; calling for the group to give or do more in return for help. Remember: exchange-based criticisms focus on the economic implications and perceived unfairness in resource distribution (difference from compassionate anti-solidarity).
    GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Points to an out-group characterised by pronounced differences, and an expression of exclusion. This can be expressed in: a stronger emphasis on one's own group's interests (e.g., We must stand up for our own interests...); resistance to support of, assimilation or integration with other groups. With migrants, this can be expressed as an emphasis on protecting interests of the native population at the expense of including migrants; portrayal of migrants as fundamentally different and unable to integrate, thus excluding them; a call for migrants to conform to existing norms without offering them full participation in society.
    COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY: It is about disregarding and excluding individuals or subgroups, refusing to support them either on the basis that they are perceived to be in a good position already, or are viewed as undeserving of help. When applied to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): denial of systemic issues (protections or affirmative actions are unnecessary or overreaching); security concerns, skepticism about the motives and legitimacy of asylum seekers;  discourse shifts focus from societal or structural solutions to individual responsibility; criticism of preferential treatment. Remember, compassionate criticisms might question the necessity of certain protections or the legitimacy of migrants (difference from exchange-based anti-solidarity).

Es liegt doch in unser aller Interesse, dass so etwas verhindert wird. Ich sage auch sehr deutlich: Es hat zu lange gedauert, bis es in Deutschland möglich war, eine tabufreie Diskussion über misslungene Integration von Zuwanderern zu führen. Die Bevölkerung spürt seit langem, dass vor dem Hintergrund des Ideals eines friedlichen Zusammenlebens von deutschen und zugewanderten Gesellschaftsteilen eine ungute Entwicklung im Gange ist. Gerade an dieser Stelle wurde von uns immer wieder darauf hingewiesen, dass es einen untrennbaren Zusammenhang zwischen innerer Sicherheit und Zuwanderung gibt. Deshalb müssen wir uns verstärkt und ohne ideologische Verbrämung fragen, wie viel Unterschiedlichkeit ein Land verträgt und wie viel Gemeinsamkeit es braucht, um seine innere Bindungskraft und seine Widerstandsfähigkeit gegenüber extremistischen Strömungen – egal woher sie kommen – nicht zu verlieren. Ich glaube, wir müssen Verpflichtungen benennen, die eine selbstbewusste, an sich glaubende, ihre kulturellen und christlich-jüdischen Grundlagen nicht negierende Aufnahmegesellschaft von denen verlangen muss, die als Neubürger aus nicht europäischen Kulturkreisen zugewandert sind und auf Dauer in unserer Mitte leben wollen. Aus dieser Debatte, Herr Bundesinnenminister, müssen sich auch Konsequenzen für die innere Sicherheit ergeben.

Explanation: The provided German text expresses concerns over the integration of migrants and the potential impacts on the inner security and social cohesion of the country. It calls for an open discussion without taboos regarding the integration of immigrants and emphasizes the need for a balance between diversity and commonality to maintain societal resilience against extremism.

The speaker acknowledges a connection between immigration and internal security, suggesting that greater scrutiny and certain expectations should be placed on non-European migrants that want to become permanent members of society. The text also implies that the host society should not have to compromise its cultural and Judeo-Christian foundations.

This message fits the ANTI-SOLIDARITY category as it emphasizes the perceived challenges associated with immigration, suggesting a need for migrants to adapt to the existing cultural norms of the host society. Given the focus on preserving the host society's culture and values while calling for migrants to meet specific obligations, the subtype that best fits this text is GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY. The text conveys the idea that migrants must conform to existing societal norms and there is a strong emphasis on the interests of the host society.

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY

Wenn der Volksmund von Obdachlosigkeit spricht, ist meist die Straßenobdachlosigkeit gemeint. Das sind die Menschen, die ohne jede Unterkunft auf der Straße leben und im Alltag in den Städten zu sehen sind. Die BAG schätzt die Anzahl dieser Personen auf etwa 52 000. Diese Zahl ist in den letzten Jahren gestiegen – das haben wir gehört –, doch auch der Anstieg der von Straßenobdachlosigkeit betroffenen Personen ist durch Migration entstanden. Der Anteil von EU-Bürgern an den Personen ohne jede Unterkunft auf der Straße beträgt laut BAG bis zu 50 Prozent. Das ist erschreckend. Der Teil des Problems der Wohnungslosigkeit, der auf Zuwanderung beruht – und das ist der Großteil –, ist meines Erachtens daher stark vom Rest der Wohnungslosigkeit zu unterscheiden, sowohl was die Gründe für die Wohnungslosigkeit als auch die Lösungsansätze betrifft.

Explanation: The provided text talks about homelessness (Obdachlosigkeit) and indicates that the issue has been affected by migration. The text specifically states that up to 50% of those without shelter are EU citizens, which it characterizes as alarming. It goes on to suggest that the portion of homelessness caused by immigration should be distinctly separated from other homelessness regarding both its causes and solutions.

The text presents an expression that distinguishes migrants (in this context, specifically homeless EU citizens) as contributing to a problem (homelessness) and suggests that these migrants should be considered differently from non-migrant homeless individuals. This indicates a stance of differentiation based on the origin of the homeless person and implies that the issue of migrant homelessness may require different or separate approaches.

The high-level category that best fits this text is ANTI-SOLIDARITY because it emphasizes a distinction between migrants and non-migrants in the context of homelessness and implies a problem associated with the migrant group. It reflects a notion that the migrant group is contributing differently to the issue and thus separates their situation from the general homelessness problem.

To specify further, this would fall under the subtype of GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY. The text shows a tendency to view the group of migrants as fundamentally different in the context of homelessness and suggests isolating the issue from the domestic context. There’s an implied perspective that may lead to the exclusion of this group from the general population experiencing homelessness.

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY

Remember, compassionate criticisms might question the necessity of certain protections or the legitimacy of migrants (difference from exchange-based anti-solidarity).

Danach soll künftig nicht mehr das Land in der EU, das ein Migrant zuerst betritt, für dessen Asylverfahren zuständig sein, sondern das Land, zu dem bereits Verbindungen bestehen. Solche Verbindungen können aufgrund früherer Aufenthalte bestehen oder dadurch, dass sich bereits ein Angehöriger im Land befindet. Nach Parlamentsvorschlägen soll „faktisch die bloße Behauptung einer Familienverbindung ausreichen“. Zitat: Wenn jeder der über 1,4 Millionen Menschen, die seit 2015 in Deutschland Asyl beantragt haben, zur Ankerperson für neu in der EU ankommende … Migranten wird, reden wir über ganz andere Größenordnungen als bei der Familienzusammenführung. So Innenstaatssekretär Ole Schröder, CDU. Und was machen die Abgeordneten der CDU/CSU, SPD und FDP im Europaparlament? Sie stimmen dieser vollkommen irrsinnigen Regel zu und dürfen sich bald als Garanten einer neuen Völkerwanderung nach Deutschland feiern lassen.

Explanation: The provided German text criticizes a proposed change in the EU asylum policy that would shift responsibility from the country of first entry to the country where the migrant has existing connections. The text quotes Innenstaatssekretär Ole Schröder of the CDU party expressing concern about the potential scale of migration due to the rule change and implies that the rule is absurd ("vollkommen irrsinnigen Regel") and refers to the support of the rule by German MEPs across different political parties sarcastically, suggesting that they should be celebrated for enabling a "new Völkerwanderung" (mass migration) to Germany.

The tone of the text is indicative of opposition to the policy change and suggests a view of migrants as a burden due to the potential increase in numbers. It also includes a sarcastic comment about MEPs being celebrated for their support of the policy, which adds a critical tone towards the idea of increasing migration. The text does not express solidarity with migrants but rather concern about the implications of the policy for the number of migrants entering Germany.

The rhetoric leans towards GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY as it implies resistance to the support of migrants by highlighting the interests of the native population that could be affected by a significant increase in migration numbers. The reference to a "new Völkerwanderung" can also be interpreted as a portrayal of migrants as a mass phenomenon that could lead to a fundamental change in the host society, which is to be prevented.

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY

Analyze the following German text and classify it into one of the high-level categories regarding migrants (refugees ― Flüchtlinge, expellees ― Vertriebene, asylum seekers ― Asylbewerber, immigrants ― Einwanderer, and other migrant categories within Germany): SOLIDARITY, ANTI-SOLIDARITY, MIXED, or NONE. If applicable, further specify by choosing the most appropriate subtype (EMPATHIC, EXCHANGE-BASED, GROUP-BASED, COMPASSIONATE) within SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Begin your response by providing the high-level category and then the subtype, if applicable.
    SOLIDARITY: Involves expressions that promote understanding, support, and unity with different groups or individuals (migrants in our case), often emphasizing shared goals, compassion, mutual assistance, and empathic understanding. Consider cases with even slight expressions of solidarity, regardless of the main topic of the text.
    ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Entails expressions that show opposition, disregard, or exclusion towards certain groups or individuals (migrants in our case). This includes emphasizing differences, denying the need for support or assistance, highlighting unequal exchanges between groups, and disregarding the unique characteristics or needs of certain groups. Even slight expressions of anti-solidarity should be considered, irrespective of the primary focus of the text.
    MIXED: A mixed stance toward migrants is characterized by the presence of both supportive and opposing expressions within the same text. This stance emerges in discussions where acknowledgment of migrants' rights, contributions, or needs is juxtaposed with limitations, conditions, or reservations that counteract or diminish the initial support. Key features of a mixed stance include (but are not limited with): conditional hospitality and selective support; balanced policies (e.g. improve the situation of migrants already within the country, while simultaneously seeking to regulate or limit further influx); expressions of empathy or concern for migrants' hardships, contrasted with discussions on practical constraints, such as societal integration challenges, or national security concerns.
    NONE: Texts which neither express solidarity nor anti-solidarity toward migrants in Germany, reflecting a neutral position or the absence of any specific stance. The absence of overt support or opposition does not automatically lead to a NONE classification; subtle cues or implicit messages may still align with solidarity or anti-solidarity categories.

    If the text falls into SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY, please specify further by choosing the most appropriate subtype from the following, after the initial high-level classification:
    
    For SOLIDARITY: EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY, GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY: Is coded when a group is different from others and this should be recognized, supported, valued. In applying empathic solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): recognition of diversity and individuality (cultural, linguistic, and personal, etc.); emphasis on the importance of preserving migrants' identities when integrating them into new communities; advocating for the right to live authentically without fear of persecution or discrimination; challenging stereotypes, prejudices against migrants; willingness to shape a diverse and dynamic community.
    EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY: Is coded when a speaker refers to the usefulness of ‘exchange partners’ in terms of their actual or future contributions (economic, cultural, or social, etc.) or willingness to contribute. In applying exchange-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): emphasis on the importance of migrants' work, skills, and cultural diversity as essential for the host society; support for migrants which is framed as an investment in individuals who contribute to the community; conditional hospitality.
    GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY: Is coded when solidarity is based on the idea of unity and support among members of a group, driven by shared characteristics, goals, interests, values and norms, or common rights and duties. The support might be driven by shared characteristics or challenges aiming at broader societal change; fostering inclusivity, equality, societal cohesion (difference from compassionate solidarity). In applying group-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): a unified effort to address and advocate for migrants' rights, equality, and representation; advocacy aimed at ensuring migrants' full integration; active stance against discrimination and xenophobia.
    COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY: Emphasizes providing support to marginalized, disadvantaged, or vulnerable groups, focusing on aid without expecting anything in return. It involves recognizing vulnerabilities, advocating for assistance to alleviate hardships, and offering support purely based on need. While not all indicators must be present, the core of compassionate solidarity lies in acknowledging and addressing the needs of those in vulnerable positions. The support is aimed at addressing vulnerabilities and needs without expectation of reciprocity; alleviating suffering and providing assistance based on need (difference from group-based solidarity).
    
    For ANTI-SOLIDARITY: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Refers to the cases when a speaker refers to a group indicating that this group is different from everyone else, but this difference should not be respected or recognised. This can be expressed in: an overt or covert refusal to acknowledge the validity of a group's diverse identities, roles, and choices. Opposition to the group's efforts to define their identity, roles, and aspirations on their own terms. Promotion of a return to or maintenance of traditional societal roles and structures that restrict the group's opportunities.
    EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Focuses on the idea that some groups take more than they give. It brings attention to groups that seem to receive a lot of resources or support, but do not contribute much or cause problems. This perspective supports punishing such groups, giving them less help, or making them contribute more. This can be expressed in suggesting that this group should be punished, or get less than they are currently getting; calling for the group to give or do more in return for help. Remember: exchange-based criticisms focus on the economic implications and perceived unfairness in resource distribution (difference from compassionate anti-solidarity).
    GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Points to an out-group characterised by pronounced differences, and an expression of exclusion. This can be expressed in: a stronger emphasis on one's own group's interests (e.g., We must stand up for our own interests...); resistance to support of, assimilation or integration with other groups. With migrants, this can be expressed as an emphasis on protecting interests of the native population at the expense of including migrants; portrayal of migrants as fundamentally different and unable to integrate, thus excluding them; a call for migrants to conform to existing norms without offering them full participation in society.
    COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY: It is about disregarding and excluding individuals or subgroups, refusing to support them either on the basis that they are perceived to be in a good position already, or are viewed as undeserving of help. When applied to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): denial of systemic issues (protections or affirmative actions are unnecessary or overreaching); security concerns, skepticism about the motives and legitimacy of asylum seekers;  discourse shifts focus from societal or structural solutions to individual responsibility; criticism of preferential treatment. Remember, compassionate criticisms might question the necessity of certain protections or the legitimacy of migrants (difference from exchange-based anti-solidarity).

Please choose just one label.

Die AfD sagt: Migration ist nicht per se positiv. Migration muss unseren Interessen dienen. Das Asylrecht ist ein Hilfsrecht, ja. Aber Migration hat den deutschen Interessen zu dienen. Dieser Pakt dient nicht unseren Interessen. Dieser Pakt ist die größte Gefahr, die sogar Sie in dieser Legislatur heraufbeschworen haben. Das will ganz schön viel heißen.

Explanation: The input text suggests a stance that is critical of migration policies that do not align with "German interests." The writer expresses a view that migration should serve the interests of the native population and positions the unspecified "Pakt" (likely referring to a migration-related pact or agreement) as against those interests and as a significant threat. Based on this analysis, the text demonstrates a disposition towards the interests of the native population over the inclusion and support of migrants, without emphasizing any form of compassionate or empathetic understanding towards the migrant group.

Therefore, the high-level category for this text is ANTI-SOLIDARITY. To further specify the subtype, the text illustrates a group-based perspective since it focuses on the importance of protecting the interests of the native group (likely German citizens) and implies exclusion or limited support for migrants, who are not perceived as part of this in-group.

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY

Ein kurzes Wort noch zu der Flüchtlingssituation in Berlin. Ich will diese Frage keineswegs dramatisieren. Aber wir sehen doch mit großer Sorge, daß Berlin durch die im Notaufnahmeverfahren abgelehnten Zuwanderer außerordentlich stark belastet ist. Im Augenblick befinden sich in Berlin zirka 40 000 nicht anerkannte Flüchtlinge, und jeden Monat kommen noch ungefähr 2500 bis 3000 abgelehnte Flüchtlinge dazu, die in Berlin bleiben. Ich glaube, ich brauche über die Situation dieser nicht anerkannten Flüchtlinge 'in Berlin nichts zu sagen. Darüber 'ist in diesem Hause schon des öfteren gesprochen worden. Ihre Lage ist denkbar ungünstig.

Explanation: The input text expresses concern over the burden faced by Berlin due to the presence of rejected asylum seekers (abgelehnten Zuwanderer) who continue to stay in the city. The use of phrases such as "großer Sorge" (great concern) and "außerordentlich stark belastet" (extraordinarily heavily burdened) indicates a perspective focused on the challenges and difficulties that the situation imposes on the host community, rather than on the hardships faced by the migrants themselves.

The speaker does not dramatize the issue, indicating a measured approach, but the language used does not convey empathy or support for the migrants. Instead, it highlights the strain on resources and the increasing numbers of non-recognized refugees as a problem. There is no mention of any positive contributions by these individuals or any show of support or understanding for their plight.

The text leans towards a stance of ANTI-SOLIDARITY because it focuses on the negative aspects of the migrant situation without expressing empathy or support. The mention of "nicht anerkannte Flüchtlinge" (non-recognized refugees) and the concern for the city's burden could suggest GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, where there is a perceived distinction between the interests of the host community and the migrants.

However, the text does not strongly express a call for exclusion or directly refuse the possibility of integration. It also does not emphasize any economic implications or unfairness in resource distribution that is characteristic of EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY. The tone is not inherently punitive or harsh, nor does it question the legitimacy of migrants or imply that they do not deserve help, which would be typical of COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Therefore, the closest subtype, albeit not perfectly fitting, might be GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, as it hints at a prioritization of the host community's interests and challenges posed by the presence of migrants without a clear solution or support offered.

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY

Das Grundrecht auf Asyl hat eben die Konsequenz, dass wir wie kein anderes Land in Europa von Zuwanderung durch Asylbewerber betroffen sind, die kein Recht haben, sich auf das Asylrecht zu berufen. Das ist das Thema. Frau Staatssekretärin, damit es da keine Unklarheiten gibt: Die Frage, wie das Problem der Zuwanderung im neuen Verfassungsvertrag zu behandeln ist – das steht auch in dem Artikel der „Rheinischen Post“, und zwar ganz am Ende –, ist zwischen CDU und CSU, auch in unserer Fraktion, völlig unstreitig. Wir sind grundsätzlich dafür, dass das in die nationale Zuständigkeit zurückgeführt wird, weil mit diesem Verfassungsvertrag auch eine klare Kompetenzabgrenzung bezweckt war und nicht zuletzt auch deshalb, weil sich gerade in Deutschland das Problem der Zuwanderung mit einer Schärfe stellt, wie das in keinem anderen Land der Fall ist. Deswegen geht es bei der Frage, wie man den Verfassungsvertrag sieht, auch ein bisschen darum, nationale Interessen wahrzunehmen. Auch den Blick dafür sollten wir nicht verlieren. Es ist zu befürchten, dass sich der deutsche Innenminister heute im Innenministerrat wieder an einem schlechten Schauspiel beteiligt, bei dem in Wahrheit die Grünen Regie führen.

Explanation: The input text discusses the issue of asylum and immigration in Germany, referencing the nation's constitutional considerations and national interests. The speaker expresses concern about the consequences of the current asylum laws and suggests that immigration should be returned to national jurisdiction. There is mention of the problem of immigration being particularly acute in Germany compared to other countries and the desire to protect national interests, potentially implying a perceived negative impact from current immigration practices.

The text does not express outright anti-migrant sentiment, but it does highlight a need for tighter national controls and seems to question the current state of immigration law. There is an implied wish to rethink how immigration and asylum are handled to better serve national interests.

Identifying a subtype within the ANTI-SOLIDARITY category, the text leans toward GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY. This is interpreted from the focus on maintaining national jurisdiction over immigration and asylum policies and the perceived challenge immigration poses to the country, hinting at placing national group interests above the interests of migrants.

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY

Analyze the following German text and classify it into one of the high-level categories regarding migrants (refugees ― Flüchtlinge, expellees ― Vertriebene, asylum seekers ― Asylbewerber, immigrants ― Einwanderer, and other migrant categories within Germany): SOLIDARITY, ANTI-SOLIDARITY, MIXED, or NONE. If applicable, further specify by choosing the most appropriate subtype (EMPATHIC, EXCHANGE-BASED, GROUP-BASED, COMPASSIONATE) within SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Begin your response by providing the high-level category and then the subtype, if applicable.
    SOLIDARITY: Involves expressions that promote understanding, support, and unity with different groups or individuals (migrants in our case), often emphasizing shared goals, compassion, mutual assistance, and empathic understanding. Consider cases with even slight expressions of solidarity, regardless of the main topic of the text.
    ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Entails expressions that show opposition, disregard, or exclusion towards certain groups or individuals (migrants in our case). This includes emphasizing differences, denying the need for support or assistance, highlighting unequal exchanges between groups, and disregarding the unique characteristics or needs of certain groups. Even slight expressions of anti-solidarity should be considered, irrespective of the primary focus of the text.
    MIXED: A mixed stance toward migrants is characterized by the presence of both supportive and opposing expressions within the same text. This stance emerges in discussions where acknowledgment of migrants' rights, contributions, or needs is juxtaposed with limitations, conditions, or reservations that counteract or diminish the initial support. Key features of a mixed stance include (but are not limited with): conditional hospitality and selective support; balanced policies (e.g. improve the situation of migrants already within the country, while simultaneously seeking to regulate or limit further influx); expressions of empathy or concern for migrants' hardships, contrasted with discussions on practical constraints, such as societal integration challenges, or national security concerns.
    NONE: Texts which neither express solidarity nor anti-solidarity toward migrants in Germany, reflecting a neutral position or the absence of any specific stance. The absence of overt support or opposition does not automatically lead to a NONE classification; subtle cues or implicit messages may still align with solidarity or anti-solidarity categories.

    If the text falls into SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY, please specify further by choosing the most appropriate subtype from the following, after the initial high-level classification:
    
    For SOLIDARITY: EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY, GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY: Is coded when a group is different from others and this should be recognized, supported, valued. In applying empathic solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): recognition of diversity and individuality (cultural, linguistic, and personal, etc.); emphasis on the importance of preserving migrants' identities when integrating them into new communities; advocating for the right to live authentically without fear of persecution or discrimination; challenging stereotypes, prejudices against migrants; willingness to shape a diverse and dynamic community.
    EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY: Is coded when a speaker refers to the usefulness of ‘exchange partners’ in terms of their actual or future contributions (economic, cultural, or social, etc.) or willingness to contribute. In applying exchange-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): emphasis on the importance of migrants' work, skills, and cultural diversity as essential for the host society; support for migrants which is framed as an investment in individuals who contribute to the community; conditional hospitality.
    GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY: Is coded when solidarity is based on the idea of unity and support among members of a group, driven by shared characteristics, goals, interests, values and norms, or common rights and duties. The support might be driven by shared characteristics or challenges aiming at broader societal change; fostering inclusivity, equality, societal cohesion (difference from compassionate solidarity). In applying group-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): a unified effort to address and advocate for migrants' rights, equality, and representation; advocacy aimed at ensuring migrants' full integration; active stance against discrimination and xenophobia.
    COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY: Emphasizes providing support to marginalized, disadvantaged, or vulnerable groups, focusing on aid without expecting anything in return. It involves recognizing vulnerabilities, advocating for assistance to alleviate hardships, and offering support purely based on need. While not all indicators must be present, the core of compassionate solidarity lies in acknowledging and addressing the needs of those in vulnerable positions. The support is aimed at addressing vulnerabilities and needs without expectation of reciprocity; alleviating suffering and providing assistance based on need (difference from group-based solidarity).
    
    For ANTI-SOLIDARITY: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Refers to the cases when a speaker refers to a group indicating that this group is different from everyone else, but this difference should not be respected or recognised. This can be expressed in: an overt or covert refusal to acknowledge the validity of a group's diverse identities, roles, and choices. Opposition to the group's efforts to define their identity, roles, and aspirations on their own terms. Promotion of a return to or maintenance of traditional societal roles and structures that restrict the group's opportunities.
    EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Focuses on the idea that some groups take more than they give. It brings attention to groups that seem to receive a lot of resources or support, but do not contribute much or cause problems. This perspective supports punishing such groups, giving them less help, or making them contribute more. This can be expressed in suggesting that this group should be punished, or get less than they are currently getting; calling for the group to give or do more in return for help. Remember: exchange-based criticisms focus on the economic implications and perceived unfairness in resource distribution (difference from compassionate anti-solidarity).
    GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Points to an out-group characterised by pronounced differences, and an expression of exclusion. This can be expressed in: a stronger emphasis on one's own group's interests (e.g., We must stand up for our own interests...); resistance to support of, assimilation or integration with other groups. With migrants, this can be expressed as an emphasis on protecting interests of the native population at the expense of including migrants; portrayal of migrants as fundamentally different and unable to integrate, thus excluding them; a call for migrants to conform to existing norms without offering them full participation in society.
    COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY: It is about disregarding and excluding individuals or subgroups, refusing to support them either on the basis that they are perceived to be in a good position already, or are viewed as undeserving of help. When applied to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): denial of systemic issues (protections or affirmative actions are unnecessary or overreaching); security concerns, skepticism about the motives and legitimacy of asylum seekers;  discourse shifts focus from societal or structural solutions to individual responsibility; criticism of preferential treatment. Remember, compassionate criticisms might question the necessity of certain protections or the legitimacy of migrants (difference from exchange-based anti-solidarity).
    
    Think step by step. Begin by analyzing the text to identify its high-level category related to migrants: SOLIDARITY, ANTI-SOLIDARITY, MIXED, or NONE. If the text falls under SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY, further specify by identifying the most appropriate subtype: EMPATHIC, EXCHANGE-BASED, GROUP-BASED, or COMPASSIONATE. After your comprehensive analysis, conclude with 'Label:' followed by the most fitting category name. If identifying a subtype, combine it with the high-level category, such as GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY, GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Please choose just one label.

Meine Damen und Herren! Liebe Kolleginnen und Kollegen! Die Zeit drängt. Die Union ist fest entschlossen, den weiteren hohen Zuzug von Asylbewerbern und Flüchtlingen durch wirksame Maßnahmen spürbar zu verringern, damit unser Land nicht überfordert wird. National und international ist vieles schon auf den Weg gebracht worden; die Bundeskanzlerin hat in ihrer Regierungserklärung die wichtigsten Punkte ja auch angesprochen. Auch auf europäischer Ebene, auch beim Europäischen Rat werden wichtige Themen erörtert. Natürlich sind die gravierenden Mängel bei der Sicherung der EU-Außengrenze zu beheben.

Explanation: The input text expresses a view that seeks to reduce the number of asylum seekers and refugees entering the country to prevent overburdening the nation. The stance emphasizes the need for effective measures to lessen the influx and discusses the importance of securing EU external borders, suggesting a focus on national interests and regulation of migration.

This perspective aligns with GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, as it places a stronger emphasis on the interests of the country's current population. It supports the approach of managing and limiting the inclusion of migrants, hinting at the portrayal of migrants as a group that might pose challenges to integration or societal cohesion.

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY

Es ist von der Regierung gesagt worden, daß die Zulassung der Ausländer zum Universitätsstudium Sache der Eiuzelstaaten sei. Danach habe ich gar nicht gefragt; ich habe nach einer reichsgesetzlichen Regelung der Ausländerfrage verlangt, und die ist gerade so gut möglich, wie die ärztliche Prüfungsordnung, die tierärztliche Prüfungsordnung reichsgesktzlich geregelt sind, wie die ReichSschulkommisston verbindlich ist für das Einjährigenexamen des ganzen Reichs. Wenn man allerdings von der Ausliinderfrage spricht, so ist das etwas euphemistisch ausgedrückt; näher kommen wir schon der Sache, wenn wir von der Rufsenfrage sprechen, und noch näher, wenn wir von der Judenfrage an den deutschen Universitäten reden. Denn es handelt sich bei den lästigen Ausländern nicht um Schweizer, Franzosen, nicht um unsere germanischen Stammesgenoffen aus dem Norden, sondern um jüdische Leute aus Rußland, die mit durchaus ungenügender Vorbildung an unsere Universitäten kommen, den deutschen Studenten die besten Plätze wegnehmen usw. Wir brauchen daher nicht nur einen Grenzschutz für unsere Tiere, sondern auch vor allen Dingen einen Grenzschutz für den deutschen Menschen. Die Herren zur Linken sollten doch vor allen Dingen den Standpunkt hochhalten, daß auch hierbei in erster Linie die Interessen des Deutschtums in Frage kommen. Es wird ihnen ja auch gestattet, das zu tun.

Explanation: The input text is expressing opposition to the presence of certain foreigners, specifically Jewish people from Russia, at German universities, suggesting that they are unqualified and taking places away from German students. The text calls for protection of German interests and, implicitly, for exclusionary measures against those non-German individuals.

The statement suggests a preference for the interests of native Germans over those of the migrants and a concern for these migrants taking resources (in this case, university places) from the native population. It implies that these migrants are not integrating well and are different from the desired migrants, such as "Swiss, French, or Nordic kin."

Based on this analysis, the high-level category of the text is ANTI-SOLIDARITY, as it expresses opposition and exclusion towards the specific group of migrants mentioned. Further specifying, the text fits within the subtype of GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, as it emphasizes the interests of the in-group ('Deutschtum', or German-ness) over those of the out-group (Jewish individuals from Russia) and supports the idea of exclusion.

 Label: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY
