Nachdem durch die Neufassung der Verordnung Vertriebene neu in die Härtefondsregelung einbezogen worden waren, mußte denjenigen Antragstellern, die die Beihilfe zum Lebensunterhalt wegen Erwerbsunfähigkeit jetzt beantragen können, eine neue Antragsfrist eröffnet werden, wenn die im § 265 Abs. 4 des Gesetzes bestimmten Fristen bereits abgelaufen waren. Dies ist in § 9 Abs. 2 der Verordnung geschehen. Die Beihilfe kann noch bis zum 31. Mai 1965 beantragt werden. Die Bestimmungen über die besondere laufende Beihilfe in § 301 a Abs. 3 LAG und in der Verordnung machen es möglich, daß Sowjetzonenflüchtlinge, die bisher wegen Überschreitens des Einkommenshöchstbetrages keine Beihilfe zum Lebensunterhalt erhalten konnten, nunmehr die besondere laufende Beihilfe beziehen. Für einen Teil der Sowjetzonenflüchtlinge, die den Antrag auf Grund von Erwerbsunfähigkeit stellen können, war die Antragsfrist des § 265 Abs. 4 LAG gleichfalls bereits abgelaufen. Für diese Fälle wurde in § 9 Abs. 4 der Verordnung ebenfalls eine neue Antragsfrist eröffnet, die gleichfalls bis zum 31. Mai 1965 läuft. Es gibt in der 2 LeistungsDV-LA danach zwar unterschiedliche Antragsfristen, die Unterschiede bestimmen sich aber nicht danach, ob ein Antragsteller Vertriebener oder Sowjetzonenflüchtling ist, sondern nach sachlichen Gesichtspunkten einheitlich für diese beiden Geschädigtengruppen.

Explanation: The input text describes administrative procedures and legislative amendments that affect expellees and refugees, specifically from the Soviet zone (Sowjetzonenflüchtlinge). It outlines the inclusion of expellees in a specific hardship fund regulation (Härtefondsregelung) and provides for new application deadlines for those eligible due to disability to apply for subsistence assistance. The text also states that these regulatory provisions apply equally to expellees and Soviet zone refugees based on substantive criteria rather than group identity.

From this analysis, the text does not express a clear attitude of support or opposition toward migrants. It is an objective account of legal and administrative regulations aimed at treating different groups of migrants equally under the law. Therefore, the text neither promotes understanding and unity (SOLIDARITY) nor shows opposition or exclusion (ANTI-SOLIDARITY) towards these groups.

In applying group-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): a unified effort to address and advocate for migrants' rights, equality, and representation; advocacy aimed at ensuring migrants' full integration; active stance against discrimination and xenophobia.
    COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY: Emphasizes providing support to marginalized, disadvantaged, or vulnerable groups, focusing on aid without expecting anything in return. It involves recognizing vulnerabilities, advocating for assistance to alleviate hardships, and offering support purely based on need. While not all indicators must be present, the core of compassionate solidarity lies in acknowledging and addressing the needs of those in vulnerable positions. The support is aimed at addressing vulnerabilities and needs without expectation of reciprocity; alleviating suffering and providing assistance based on need (difference from group-based solidarity).
    
    For ANTI-SOLIDARITY: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Refers to the cases when a speaker refers to a group indicating that this group is different from everyone else, but this difference should not be respected or recognised. This can be expressed in: an overt or covert refusal to acknowledge the validity of a group's diverse identities, roles, and choices. Opposition to the group's efforts to define their identity, roles, and aspirations on their own terms. Promotion of a return to or maintenance of traditional societal roles and structures that restrict the group's opportunities.
    EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Focuses on the idea that some groups take more than they give. It brings attention to groups that seem to receive a lot of resources or support, but do not contribute much or cause problems. This perspective supports punishing such groups, giving them less help, or making them contribute more. This can be expressed in suggesting that this group should be punished, or get less than they are currently getting; calling for the group to give or do more in return for help. Remember: exchange-based criticisms focus on the economic implications and perceived unfairness in resource distribution (difference from compassionate anti-solidarity).
    GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Points to an out-group characterised by pronounced differences, and an expression of exclusion. This can be expressed in: a stronger emphasis on one's own group's interests (e.g., We must stand up for our own interests...); resistance to support of, assimilation or integration with other groups. With migrants, this can be expressed as an emphasis on protecting interests of the native population at the expense of including migrants; portrayal of migrants as fundamentally different and unable to integrate, thus excluding them; a call for migrants to conform to existing norms without offering them full participation in society.
    COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY: It is about disregarding and excluding individuals or subgroups, refusing to support them either on the basis that they are perceived to be in a good position already, or are viewed as undeserving of help. When applied to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): denial of systemic issues (protections or affirmative actions are unnecessary or overreaching); security concerns, skepticism about the motives and legitimacy of asylum seekers;  discourse shifts focus from societal or structural solutions to individual responsibility; criticism of preferential treatment. Remember, compassionate criticisms might question the necessity of certain protections or the legitimacy of migrants (difference from exchange-based anti-solidarity).

Please choose just one label.

Hand hat, ob und wann er sich hier wieder betreffen lasten will, eine schwere Versuchung und Verlockung ist, denen er kaum widerstehen wird, sein Vermögen geringer anzugeben, als es wirklich ist. Meine Herren, das Gesetz thut nicht gut, eine solche Versuchung dem Schuldner nahe zu legen, Und sie ist ihm nahe gelegt, und der ausländische Schuldner wird dieser Versuchung, wenn er nicht ein äußerst redlicher Mann ist, sehr leicht unterliegen und zwar viel leichter Sie werden das einräumen muffen als der inländische Schuldner, der dem Richter erreichbar bleibt. Der ausländische Schuldner wird dieser Versuchung auch darum viel leichter unterliegen, weil der Manifestations-Eid die promissorische Clausei enthalt, alles etwa Vergessene nachträglich getreulich anzugeben, und Sie wissen, meine Herren, das Gewissen dürfte nur wenig elastisch sein, um in dieser Clausel Alles zu verbergen, was dem eingereichten Vermögens-Verzeichnisse an Vollständigkeit abgeht. Ach Lin daher der Meinung, meine Herren, daß, wenn auch zum Theil und in vielen Fällen praktisch durch das von der Commission angenommene Amendement geholfen sein wird, doch im Allgemeinen und Großen das Princip richtig war, welches ein in der Commission eingebrachter, jedoch dort wieder zurückgenommener Vorschlag enthielt, das Princip: der § 1 findet aus Ausländer keine Anwendung, insoweit nicht die inländischen Erkenntnisse auch in den ausländischen Staaten vollstreckbar sind. Meine Herren, dieser Gedanke war meines Erachtens sehr richtig und derjenige, welcher das Kind, wenn ich mich so ausdrücken darf, beim rechten Namen nennt. Denn, meine Herren, der Vorschlag, den Ihre Commission angenommen hat, und wodurch die Sache gewissermaßen hinten herum wieder ins Gleichgewicht gebracht werden soll, vermeidet es nur, das Kind beim rechten Namen zu nennen und kann doch nicht umhin, ausdrücklich von den „nicht zum Norddeutschen Bunde Gehörigen '', also von Fremden, zu sprechen; es bleibt also ziemlich dasselbe. Meine Herren, das sind die Gründe, weshalb meiner Anficht nach bei Nichtangehörigen des Norddeutschen Bundes, bei Fremden, zur Aushebung der Personal-Exemtion überhaupt kein Grund vorliegt.

It further delves into the implementation issues of a specific legal amendment related to creditors and debt enforcement, and it voices a preference for a legal principle that exempts foreigners from certain laws unless domestic legal recognition is in place in foreign states.

While this text does involve the discussion of "foreigners" (ausländische Schuldner) and "non-members of the North German Confederation" (Nichtangehörigen des Norddeutschen Bundes), it primarily addresses them within the specific context of financial/legal accountability and reciprocity between states rather than in the broad context of migration policies or social attitudes toward migrants. The text does not provide indications of a stance of either solidarity or anti-solidarity with migrants as a social group, nor does it address their social, humanitarian, or political circumstances. Instead, it is focused on a specific aspect of the legal treatment of foreign debtors without expressing any social stance towards these individuals as migrants.

As a result, the text does not fit into the categories of SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY, as it does not explicitly show support or opposition toward migrants in Germany. Instead, it provides a neutral, legalistic discussion without expressing any specific stance toward the group as migrants, which indicates a classification of NONE.

Ich weiß, Sie von der CDU/CSU werden hier vor allen Dingen gleich wieder von einer fatalen Signalwirkung sprechen. Ich will Ihnen dazu sagen, dass ich das für Quatsch halte. Wer Gründe hat, zu fliehen, bleibt nicht weg, weil es hier ein Ermittlungsverfahren wegen illegalen Grenzübertritts gibt. Es gibt keinen vernünftigen Grund dafür, Flüchtlinge und ihre Helfer wegen angeblich unerlaubter Einreise zu kriminalisieren. Wer das will, dem geht es nur darum, seine Ressentiments gegen Flüchtlinge zu pflegen. Wir sagen: Entlasten wir die Polizei und die Ermittlungsbehörden, entkriminalisieren wir die Flüchtlinge, nehmen wir die Asylgarantie des Grundgesetzes ernst. Ich danke Ihnen.

Explanation: The input text expresses a clear message against the criminalization of refugees and those aiding them. It dismisses the notion that legal action against refugees has a deterrent effect. Additionally, the text advocates taking the guarantee of asylum seriously, with an emphasis on supporting refugees by reducing the burden on police and investigative authorities. The speaker addresses the audience, which seems to disagree with their viewpoint, challenging their assumed position, which seems to discourage welcoming refugees.

This message strongly conveys support for migrants (refugees in this context) and challenges those holding opposing views by calling their arguments "Quatsch" (nonsense) and accusing them of maintaining resentments against refugees. There are no explicit statements of the migrants' contributions (economical, cultural, etc.), nor are there indicators of shared group characteristics with the migrants. The focal point is the rejection of criminalizing behavior that the speaker deems to be driven by compassion and necessity (helping refugees).

The speech primarily displays an attitude of compassionate support for a vulnerable group without expectations of reciprocity or emphasis on contributions from the migrants.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

Sie hat sich im Rahmen der Kontaktgruppe intensiv für diese Verhandlungen eingesetzt, und das war richtig. Denn wir wissen - auch das gilt es auszusprechen -, daß unser Land leicht das Ziel einer großen Flüchtlingsbewegung werden könnte und immer noch werden kann. Deutschland hat - mir ist wichtig, daß das einmal festgestellt wird - im Zusammenhang mit der Bosnien-Krise bewiesen, daß es seine humanitäre Verpflichtung wirklich ernst nimmt. Wir, die Deutschen, haben - das sage ich auch unseren ausländischen Kritikern - über 300 000 Flüchtlinge aus Bosnien aufgenommen, von denen ein großer Teil inzwischen zurückkehren konnte. Vor allem auf der Ebene der Länder und der Gemeinden sind inzwischen mehr als 20 Milliarden DM aufgewendet worden, um den betroffenen Menschen zu helfen. Wir sind nicht hartherzig, wenn wir sagen, daß auch wir überfordert werden können. Darum haben gerade wir ein vitales Interesse an Frieden und Stabilität, auch und gerade auf dem Balkan.

Explanation: The input text discusses Germany's involvement in assisting refugees from the Bosnian crisis, highlighting the country's humanitarian efforts and financial contributions. It acknowledges the potential of migration challenges while also mentioning the nation's interests in peace and stability, especially in regions like the Balkans, from where the refugees are originating.

The text expresses SOLIDARITY with refugees by describing the humanitarian assistance provided to them, indicating the country's serious attitude towards its humanitarian obligations. It mentions the significant number of refugees (over 300,000) taken in from Bosnia and the considerable financial expenditure (more than 20 billion DM) to support these people. It implies a recognition of the country’s ability to become overwhelmed, highlighting a self-aware approach to its capabilities and limits regarding refugee assistance. However, there is no expression of anti-solidity, no tone of opposition or disregard towards the migrants.

The subtype that fits best here is COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. This is because the text focuses on the support and aid provided based on the needs of the refugees from Bosnia, emphasizing that the help was given without expecting anything in return. It stresses the substantial efforts to alleviate the hardships faced by these vulnerable individuals purely based on their need for assistance.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

In applying group-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): a unified effort to address and advocate for migrants' rights, equality, and representation; advocacy aimed at ensuring migrants' full integration; active stance against discrimination and xenophobia.
    COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY: Emphasizes providing support to marginalized, disadvantaged, or vulnerable groups, focusing on aid without expecting anything in return. It involves recognizing vulnerabilities, advocating for assistance to alleviate hardships, and offering support purely based on need. While not all indicators must be present, the core of compassionate solidarity lies in acknowledging and addressing the needs of those in vulnerable positions. The support is aimed at addressing vulnerabilities and needs without expectation of reciprocity; alleviating suffering and providing assistance based on need (difference from group-based solidarity).
    
    For ANTI-SOLIDARITY: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Refers to the cases when a speaker refers to a group indicating that this group is different from everyone else, but this difference should not be respected or recognised. This can be expressed in: an overt or covert refusal to acknowledge the validity of a group's diverse identities, roles, and choices. Opposition to the group's efforts to define their identity, roles, and aspirations on their own terms. Promotion of a return to or maintenance of traditional societal roles and structures that restrict the group's opportunities.
    EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Focuses on the idea that some groups take more than they give. It brings attention to groups that seem to receive a lot of resources or support, but do not contribute much or cause problems. This perspective supports punishing such groups, giving them less help, or making them contribute more. This can be expressed in suggesting that this group should be punished, or get less than they are currently getting; calling for the group to give or do more in return for help. Remember: exchange-based criticisms focus on the economic implications and perceived unfairness in resource distribution (difference from compassionate anti-solidarity).
    GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Points to an out-group characterised by pronounced differences, and an expression of exclusion. This can be expressed in: a stronger emphasis on one's own group's interests (e.g., We must stand up for our own interests...); resistance to support of, assimilation or integration with other groups. With migrants, this can be expressed as an emphasis on protecting interests of the native population at the expense of including migrants; portrayal of migrants as fundamentally different and unable to integrate, thus excluding them; a call for migrants to conform to existing norms without offering them full participation in society.
    COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY: It is about disregarding and excluding individuals or subgroups, refusing to support them either on the basis that they are perceived to be in a good position already, or are viewed as undeserving of help. When applied to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): denial of systemic issues (protections or affirmative actions are unnecessary or overreaching); security concerns, skepticism about the motives and legitimacy of asylum seekers;  discourse shifts focus from societal or structural solutions to individual responsibility; criticism of preferential treatment. Remember, compassionate criticisms might question the necessity of certain protections or the legitimacy of migrants (difference from exchange-based anti-solidarity).

Please choose just one label.

Die Innenminister haben 1999 nach dem Ende des KosovoKriegs dankenswerterweise beschlossen, dass sie nicht zurückgeschickt werden. 85 000 Flüchtlinge sind längst freiwillig zurückgekehrt. Worüber sprechen wir eigentlich? – Wir sprechen über 120 000 Flüchtlinge. Ich meine, wenn der Weg der freiwilligen Rückkehr und der Unterstützung all derer, die freiwillig zurückgehen wollen, weiterverfolgt würde, dann würde es nicht zu einer Belastung des Kosovo kommen, zu der es derzeit leicht kommen kann. Deswegen appelliere ich dringend, keine Abschiebungen durchzuführen, und zwar aus dem einfachen Grund, dass im Kosovo 50 Prozent der Menschen arbeitslos sind. Diejenigen, die Häuser besitzen, sind schließlich schon zurückgekehrt.

Explanation: The provided German text can be translated as follows:

"The interior ministers have thankfully decided in 1999, after the end of the Kosovo War, that they shall not be sent back. 85,000 refugees have already returned voluntarily. What are we actually talking about? – We are talking about 120,000 refugees. I mean, if the path of voluntary return and supporting all those who want to return voluntarily were to be continued, then it would not lead to a burden on Kosovo, which can easily happen at the moment. Therefore, I urgently appeal not to carry out deportations, simply because 50 percent of the people in Kosovo are unemployed. Those who own houses have already returned."

The high-level category that this text belongs to is SOLIDARITY with the subtype COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. The text expresses a concern over the potential hardship that forced deportations could create in Kosovo due to existing high unemployment. The appeal to continue supporting voluntary returns and to avoid deportations is based on compassion for the situation of the people in Kosovo and a consideration for their well-being.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

Meine lieben Kolleginnen und Kollegen, als Initiator des Berliner Prozesses – und das sind wir von der Bundesregierung gewesen – tut auch Deutschland gut daran, sich an dieser europäischen Mission zu beteiligen, zunächst mit einem Aufklärungsflugzeug und Stabspersonal, später aber auch mit einem Schiff der Marine. Wenn wir darüber reden, wo wir Verantwortung übernehmen können oder dass wir mehr Verantwortung auf der Welt übernehmen müssen, ist dies ein Beispiel dafür, dass wir es tun, meine sehr verehrten Damen und Herren. Das ist nicht nur ein essenzieller Beitrag zur Stabilität in unserer unmittelbaren Nachbarschaft, sondern es ist auch ein wichtiges Signal europäischer Geschlossenheit und Handlungsfähigkeit, gerade in Zeiten wie diesen. Meine Damen und Herren, bei alldem haben wir aber auch diejenigen im Blick, die am meisten unter dem Konflikt leiden: Es sind Zehntausende Flüchtlinge und die libysche Zivilbevölkerung. Über den UNHCR hat Deutschland deshalb in den letzten Jahren mehr als 40 Millionen Euro zum Schutz von Flüchtlingen zur Verfügung gestellt. Hunderte besonders Schutzbedürftige – das wird teilweise in der aufgeregten Öffentlichkeit überhaupt nicht mehr wahrgenommen – haben durch die Vermittlung des UNHCR einen sicheren Zufluchtsort in Deutschland gefunden, und die Aufnahme von 300 weiteren steht kurz bevor. Über den EU-Nothilfefonds für Afrika unterstützen wir mit mehr als 70 Millionen Euro die Arbeit von IOM und geben damit Hilfen bei der freiwilligen Rückkehr von Migranten und für Schutzmaßnahmen in Libyen.

Explanation: The input text reflects a stance of SOLIDARITY regarding migrants. It discusses Germany's efforts in contributing to stability in the European neighborhood and specifically highlights the plight of refugees and the Libyan civilian population. The text mentions significant financial support through the UNHCR for the protection of refugees and lists efforts made for the safe relocation of vulnerable individuals to Germany. Additionally, there is a mention of support for the IOM's work in providing aid for the voluntary return of migrants and protective measures in Libya. 

The text does not lean towards exchange-based solidarity, as it does not primarily focus on the contributions of refugees or migrants to the host society or reciprocity. Instead, there is a strong sense of compassionate support demonstrated by providing assistance based on need and vulnerability.

Therefore, the most appropriate subtype within the solidarity category for the input text is COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

So hat etwa der Freistaat Bayern die Sudetendeutschen als seinen vierten Stamm aufgenommen, und bis heute ist dort an vielen Stellen und Orten die Handschrift der Sudetendeutschen erkennbar. Meine Damen und Herren, mit anderen zu teilen, die in Not sind, baut auf einem geistigen Fundament und einem Menschenbild auf, das Bundeskanzler Konrad Adenauer zum Ausdruck brachte, indem er sagte: Im Mittelpunkt allen Strebens und Handelns bleibt der Mensch und seine Freiheit. – Die zweite Erkenntnis: Eine Nation ist eine Solidargemeinschaft, eine Schicksalsgemeinschaft, eine Familie – in guten wie in schlechten Zeiten. Im Bundesvertriebenengesetz wurden deshalb auch zwei Grundsätze formuliert, nämlich erstens: Vertriebene sind voll gleichberechtigte Staatsbürger der Bundesrepublik Deutschland. Zweitens: Notwendige Hilfe gibt es so lange, bis die Eingliederung in das wirtschaftliche und soziale Leben erfolgt ist. Das heißt, niemand sollte bevorzugt werden; aber es sollte sichergestellt werden, dass alle die gleichen Möglichkeiten und die gleichen Bedingungen für einen Neuanfang haben. Nicht die Umverteilung war das Ziel, sondern Ziel war es, jedem Menschen die Chance zu geben, sich selbst zu entwickeln, sich einzubringen und teilzuhaben.

Explanation: The provided German text outlines an attitude of inclusion, support, and equality towards the Sudetendeutsche (Sudeten Germans), a group of expellees and their descendants who were forced to leave their homes after World War II. The text reflects upon the integration of this group into German society, especially within the state of Bavaria. It speaks of sharing with those in need, referencing Konrad Adenauer's value on human dignity and freedom. It calls for a spirit of community and national unity, likening the nation to a family that stands together in good and bad times. The Bundesvertriebenengesetz (Federal Expellee Law) is mentioned, specifying that expellees have equal rights as citizens and are entitled to necessary assistance until their integration into economic and social life is achieved. The goal is depicted as ensuring equal opportunities for all without favoritism, emphasizing individual development and participation in society.

This text supports the group of migrants (in this case, expellees) in a way that is focused on ensuring equal and fair treatment as well as providing necessary support without biases. The statements do not demand anything in return from the migrants, nor does it offer support conditionally. Therefore, the text can be classified under the SOLIDARITY category. 

More specifically, the text can be classified as COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY since it emphasizes providing support purely based on need, without expectation of reciprocity. It acknowledges the vulnerability of the Sudetendeutsche and advocates for assistance to alleviate their hardships, aiming to equip them with equal opportunities for a new start, fostering an atmosphere of inclusivity and equality.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

    For ANTI-SOLIDARITY: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Refers to the cases when a speaker refers to a group indicating that this group is different from everyone else, but this difference should not be respected or recognised. This can be expressed in: an overt or covert refusal to acknowledge the validity of a group's diverse identities, roles, and choices. Opposition to the group's efforts to define their identity, roles, and aspirations on their own terms. Promotion of a return to or maintenance of traditional societal roles and structures that restrict the group's opportunities.
    EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Focuses on the idea that some groups take more than they give. It brings attention to groups that seem to receive a lot of resources or support, but do not contribute much or cause problems. This perspective supports punishing such groups, giving them less help, or making them contribute more. This can be expressed in suggesting that this group should be punished, or get less than they are currently getting; calling for the group to give or do more in return for help. Remember: exchange-based criticisms focus on the economic implications and perceived unfairness in resource distribution (difference from compassionate anti-solidarity).
    GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Points to an out-group characterised by pronounced differences, and an expression of exclusion. This can be expressed in: a stronger emphasis on one's own group's interests (e.g., We must stand up for our own interests...); resistance to support of, assimilation or integration with other groups. With migrants, this can be expressed as an emphasis on protecting interests of the native population at the expense of including migrants; portrayal of migrants as fundamentally different and unable to integrate, thus excluding them; a call for migrants to conform to existing norms without offering them full participation in society.
    COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY: It is about disregarding and excluding individuals or subgroups, refusing to support them either on the basis that they are perceived to be in a good position already, or are viewed as undeserving of help. When applied to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): denial of systemic issues (protections or affirmative actions are unnecessary or overreaching); security concerns, skepticism about the motives and legitimacy of asylum seekers;  discourse shifts focus from societal or structural solutions to individual responsibility; criticism of preferential treatment. Remember, compassionate criticisms might question the necessity of certain protections or the legitimacy of migrants (difference from exchange-based anti-solidarity).

If the text falls under SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY, further specify by identifying the most appropriate subtype: EMPATHIC, EXCHANGE-BASED, GROUP-BASED, or COMPASSIONATE. After your comprehensive analysis, conclude with 'Label:' followed by the most fitting category name. If identifying a subtype, combine it with the high-level category, such as GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY, GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Please choose just one label.

Bonn, Donnerstag, den 19. Januar 1995 755 Amke Dietert-Scheuer schoben, in denen Schutz vor Verfolgung nicht gesichert ist. Es besteht die akute Gefahr, daß sie durch Kettenabschiebungen wieder im Verfolgerland ankommen. Die europäischen Staaten berufen sich gerne auf ihre gemeinsamen Werte von Demokratie und Menschenrechten. Sie sind aber nicht bereit, Flüchtlinge vor Menschenrechtsverletzungen zu schützen, sondern diffamieren sie als Schmarotzer und Bedrohung unserer Sicherheit, wie dies leider auch gerade durch den Kollegen der SPD wieder geschehen ist. Der Vertrag von Maastricht sieht die gemeinsame Einführung eines Visumzwangs vor, wenn auf Grund einer Notlage in einem Land eine größere Fluchtbewegung zu erwarten ist. So wird ganz bewußt verfolgten oder unter sonstigen Notlagen leidenden Menschen die Flucht erheblich erschwert, oft sogar unmöglich gemacht. Flüchtlinge werden damit geradezu in die Arme von Schlepperorganisationen getrieben, über die man sich hier so gerne beschwert.

Explanation: The given German text discusses the plight of refugees who face the threat of being sent back to countries where they are not protected from persecution. It criticizes European states for not adhering to their proclaimed values of democracy and human rights by failing to protect refugees and instead defaming them as parasites and threats to security. The text also mentions the introduction of visa requirements in response to crises, making it even more difficult for those persecuted or suffering to seek refuge, effectively driving them into the hands of traffickers.

The text falls under the SOLIDARITY category as it expresses concerns about the treatment of refugees and the adequacy of their protection by European countries. The author criticizes measures that make it difficult for refugees to seek sanctuary and denounces the stigmatization and defamation of refugees by political figures. As the author is calling attention to the need to protect the rights and dignities of refugees, whom they see as being marginalized and in need of support, the most appropriate subtype would be COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. This subtype is characterized by emphasizing support for marginalized, disadvantaged, or vulnerable groups based on need and advocating for assistance to alleviate hardships without expecting anything in return.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

Analyze the following German text and classify it into one of the high-level categories regarding migrants (refugees ― Flüchtlinge, expellees ― Vertriebene, asylum seekers ― Asylbewerber, immigrants ― Einwanderer, and other migrant categories within Germany): SOLIDARITY, ANTI-SOLIDARITY, MIXED, or NONE. If applicable, further specify by choosing the most appropriate subtype (EMPATHIC, EXCHANGE-BASED, GROUP-BASED, COMPASSIONATE) within SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Begin your response by providing the high-level category and then the subtype, if applicable.
    SOLIDARITY: Involves expressions that promote understanding, support, and unity with different groups or individuals (migrants in our case), often emphasizing shared goals, compassion, mutual assistance, and empathic understanding. Consider cases with even slight expressions of solidarity, regardless of the main topic of the text.
    ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Entails expressions that show opposition, disregard, or exclusion towards certain groups or individuals (migrants in our case). This includes emphasizing differences, denying the need for support or assistance, highlighting unequal exchanges between groups, and disregarding the unique characteristics or needs of certain groups. Even slight expressions of anti-solidarity should be considered, irrespective of the primary focus of the text.
    MIXED: A mixed stance toward migrants is characterized by the presence of both supportive and opposing expressions within the same text. This stance emerges in discussions where acknowledgment of migrants' rights, contributions, or needs is juxtaposed with limitations, conditions, or reservations that counteract or diminish the initial support. Key features of a mixed stance include (but are not limited with): conditional hospitality and selective support; balanced policies (e.g. improve the situation of migrants already within the country, while simultaneously seeking to regulate or limit further influx); expressions of empathy or concern for migrants' hardships, contrasted with discussions on practical constraints, such as societal integration challenges, or national security concerns.
    NONE: Texts which neither express solidarity nor anti-solidarity toward migrants in Germany, reflecting a neutral position or the absence of any specific stance. The absence of overt support or opposition does not automatically lead to a NONE classification; subtle cues or implicit messages may still align with solidarity or anti-solidarity categories.

Diesen Menschen, den Ausländer- und Asylinitiativen, den karitativen Einrichtungen, Kirchen und Gewerkschaften danken wir für ihre beispielhafte humanitäre Arbeit. Sie geben j a nicht nur den betreuten Ausländern Mut, sondern auch vielen anderen Menschen, die sich feindseligen Stimmungswogen entgegenstellen und sich dabei besorgt fragen, wie lange sich schlimme Entwicklungen noch verhindern lassen. Wir müssen sie aber verhindern, auch in Zukunft und hoffentlich mit Hilfe unserer heutigen gemeinsamen Erklärung. Denn eine Bundesrepublik Deutschland, deren Rechts- und Gesellschaftsordnung und deren öffentliche Meinung von Geringschätzung und Feindseligkeit gegenüber unseren ausländischen Mitbürgern und auch den Asylsuchenden geprägt würde, wäre als freiheitlicher Rechtsstaat im Kern beschädigt. Ich danke Ihnen. Vizepräsident Westphal: Das Wort hat der Herr Abgeordnete Baum. Baum: Herr Präsident!

Explanation: The provided German text expresses gratitude towards various organizations and individuals for their humanitarian work related to foreigners and asylum seekers. It acknowledges the resilience and encouragement their work provides not only to the people they care for but also to others standing against hostile attitudes. The text emphasizes the importance of preventing negative developments towards foreign citizens and asylum seekers, stating that such an environment of disdain and hostility would compromise the core of Germany's liberal legal system.

The recognition of the humanitarian work and its necessity to maintain the values of the legal state indicate a high regard for the rights and well-being of foreigners and asylum seekers. There is a clear stance against discrimination and xenophobia, advocating for societal values that include respect and a lack of hostility towards migrants. The text appears to align with the values of inclusivity and support for individuals based on need, without indicating expectations of specific contributions from them in return.

Based on this analysis, the text falls within the SOLIDARITY category due to its positive tone towards migrants and appreciation for those supporting them. The text's focus on combating negative developments, along with its emphasis on the importance of humanitarian work, suggests a subtype of COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. This subtype is characterized by a focus on aid and advocacy for marginalized groups without an expectation of reciprocity, which mirrors the sentiments present in the text.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

Nein, wir brauchen sozialen Wohnungsbau, und ich fordere die Länder auf, die Mittel des Bundes nicht nur einzusetzen, sondern auch noch zu verstärken. So können wir gemeinsam für preiswerte, für bezahlbare Mieten in unserem Land sorgen. Ich komme zum Schluss. Der Zustrom von Flüchtlingen – der eine oder andere Redner hat es schon angesprochen – treibt uns alle, wie ich glaube, mit Sorge um. Das muss auch so sein. Ich glaube, hier stehen zuallererst die Länder, dann aber auch der Bund in der Verantwortung. Wir müssen dafür sorgen, dass diese Menschen würdig untergebracht werden, meine Damen und Herren.

Explanation: The provided German text acknowledges the need for social housing and suggests that the state (Länder) and federal (Bund) governments are responsible for ensuring affordable housing. It also mentions the influx of refugees as a matter of concern, and stresses the responsibility of the government entities to ensure that refugees are accommodated with dignity.

Analysis:
- The text does not explicitly promote opposition, disregard, or exclusion towards the group of refugees. Hence, ANTI-SOLIDARITY is not applicable.
- The text does express concern for the plight of the refugees and emphasizes the responsibilities of various levels of government to ensure dignity in their accommodation.
- Within this context, the text conveys a sense of duty and recognition of the needs of refugees.

Given this analysis, the high-level category that fits the text is SOLIDARITY. Further specifying within the subtypes:

- EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY: There is no explicit mention of celebrating diversity or individuality.
- EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY: There is no mention of refugees’ contributions or an exchange of resources or benefits.
- GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY: The text does not discuss shared goals or interests among members of a group or the broader community.
- COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY: The text emphasizes assistance based on the needs of refugees, focusing on the humane treatment and dignified accommodation, without mentioning an expectation of reciprocity or contributions.

The subtype that best fits the text is COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY because it emphasizes the provision of support to refugees based on their vulnerable position and the need for dignified treatment.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

With migrants, this can be expressed as an emphasis on protecting interests of the native population at the expense of including migrants; portrayal of migrants as fundamentally different and unable to integrate, thus excluding them; a call for migrants to conform to existing norms without offering them full participation in society.
    COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY: It is about disregarding and excluding individuals or subgroups, refusing to support them either on the basis that they are perceived to be in a good position already, or are viewed as undeserving of help. When applied to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): denial of systemic issues (protections or affirmative actions are unnecessary or overreaching); security concerns, skepticism about the motives and legitimacy of asylum seekers;  discourse shifts focus from societal or structural solutions to individual responsibility; criticism of preferential treatment. Remember, compassionate criticisms might question the necessity of certain protections or the legitimacy of migrants (difference from exchange-based anti-solidarity).

Please choose just one label.

Kerstin Müller (BÜNDNIS 90/DIE GRÜNEN): Herr Präsident! Meine Damen und Herren! Am 12. September 1995 hat Innenminister Kanther sieben Menschen in den Folterstaat Sudan abgeschoben. Jetzt sitzen schon wieder neue Flüchtlinge aus dem Sudan zusammengepfercht in den Transiträumen am Frankfurter Flughafen. Was wird mit ihnen geschehen? - Gestern ist einer abgeschoben worden. Die Absetzung der Aktuellen Stunde war aus unserer Sicht ein Fehler.

Explanation: The given German text by Kerstin Müller expresses concern over the deportation of individuals to a state where they may be subject to torture, which indicates a stance against such actions. The text highlights the case of refugees from Sudan being held at Frankfurt airport and raises questions about their fate, with one already being deported. The mention of these refugees being “zusammengepfercht” (crowded together) in transit areas suggests a criticism of their treatment, and the critique of the decision to deport these individuals implies advocacy against mistreatment and for the rights of these vulnerable groups.

This can be classified under the SOLIDARITY category, as it shows concern for the rights and well-being of migrants and opposition to their deportation to harmful environments.

Within the SOLIDARITY high-level category, the most appropriate subtype seems to be COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. The concern expressed isn't about the potential contributions of these refugees to the host society or their group identity; rather, it is about the humanitarian aspect of their situation — their potential exposure to torture upon deportation, indicating an argument against their deportation based on the potential for suffering.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

Analyze the following German text and classify it into one of the high-level categories regarding migrants (refugees ― Flüchtlinge, expellees ― Vertriebene, asylum seekers ― Asylbewerber, immigrants ― Einwanderer, and other migrant categories within Germany): SOLIDARITY, ANTI-SOLIDARITY, MIXED, or NONE. If applicable, further specify by choosing the most appropriate subtype (EMPATHIC, EXCHANGE-BASED, GROUP-BASED, COMPASSIONATE) within SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Begin your response by providing the high-level category and then the subtype, if applicable.
    SOLIDARITY: Involves expressions that promote understanding, support, and unity with different groups or individuals (migrants in our case), often emphasizing shared goals, compassion, mutual assistance, and empathic understanding. Consider cases with even slight expressions of solidarity, regardless of the main topic of the text.
    ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Entails expressions that show opposition, disregard, or exclusion towards certain groups or individuals (migrants in our case). This includes emphasizing differences, denying the need for support or assistance, highlighting unequal exchanges between groups, and disregarding the unique characteristics or needs of certain groups. Even slight expressions of anti-solidarity should be considered, irrespective of the primary focus of the text.
    MIXED: A mixed stance toward migrants is characterized by the presence of both supportive and opposing expressions within the same text. This stance emerges in discussions where acknowledgment of migrants' rights, contributions, or needs is juxtaposed with limitations, conditions, or reservations that counteract or diminish the initial support. Key features of a mixed stance include (but are not limited with): conditional hospitality and selective support; balanced policies (e.g. improve the situation of migrants already within the country, while simultaneously seeking to regulate or limit further influx); expressions of empathy or concern for migrants' hardships, contrasted with discussions on practical constraints, such as societal integration challenges, or national security concerns.
    NONE: Texts which neither express solidarity nor anti-solidarity toward migrants in Germany, reflecting a neutral position or the absence of any specific stance. The absence of overt support or opposition does not automatically lead to a NONE classification; subtle cues or implicit messages may still align with solidarity or anti-solidarity categories.

    If the text falls into SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY, please specify further by choosing the most appropriate subtype from the following, after the initial high-level classification:
    
    For SOLIDARITY: EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY, GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY: Is coded when a group is different from others and this should be recognized, supported, valued. In applying empathic solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): recognition of diversity and individuality (cultural, linguistic, and personal, etc.); emphasis on the importance of preserving migrants' identities when integrating them into new communities; advocating for the right to live authentically without fear of persecution or discrimination; challenging stereotypes, prejudices against migrants; willingness to shape a diverse and dynamic community.
    EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY: Is coded when a speaker refers to the usefulness of ‘exchange partners’ in terms of their actual or future contributions (economic, cultural, or social, etc.) or willingness to contribute. In applying exchange-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): emphasis on the importance of migrants' work, skills, and cultural diversity as essential for the host society; support for migrants which is framed as an investment in individuals who contribute to the community; conditional hospitality.
    GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY: Is coded when solidarity is based on the idea of unity and support among members of a group, driven by shared characteristics, goals, interests, values and norms, or common rights and duties. The support might be driven by shared characteristics or challenges aiming at broader societal change; fostering inclusivity, equality, societal cohesion (difference from compassionate solidarity). In applying group-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): a unified effort to address and advocate for migrants' rights, equality, and representation; advocacy aimed at ensuring migrants' full integration; active stance against discrimination and xenophobia.
    COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY: Emphasizes providing support to marginalized, disadvantaged, or vulnerable groups, focusing on aid without expecting anything in return. It involves recognizing vulnerabilities, advocating for assistance to alleviate hardships, and offering support purely based on need. While not all indicators must be present, the core of compassionate solidarity lies in acknowledging and addressing the needs of those in vulnerable positions. The support is aimed at addressing vulnerabilities and needs without expectation of reciprocity; alleviating suffering and providing assistance based on need (difference from group-based solidarity).
    
    For ANTI-SOLIDARITY: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Refers to the cases when a speaker refers to a group indicating that this group is different from everyone else, but this difference should not be respected or recognised. This can be expressed in: an overt or covert refusal to acknowledge the validity of a group's diverse identities, roles, and choices. Opposition to the group's efforts to define their identity, roles, and aspirations on their own terms. Promotion of a return to or maintenance of traditional societal roles and structures that restrict the group's opportunities.
    EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Focuses on the idea that some groups take more than they give. It brings attention to groups that seem to receive a lot of resources or support, but do not contribute much or cause problems. This perspective supports punishing such groups, giving them less help, or making them contribute more. This can be expressed in suggesting that this group should be punished, or get less than they are currently getting; calling for the group to give or do more in return for help. Remember: exchange-based criticisms focus on the economic implications and perceived unfairness in resource distribution (difference from compassionate anti-solidarity).
    GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Points to an out-group characterised by pronounced differences, and an expression of exclusion. This can be expressed in: a stronger emphasis on one's own group's interests (e.g., We must stand up for our own interests...); resistance to support of, assimilation or integration with other groups. With migrants, this can be expressed as an emphasis on protecting interests of the native population at the expense of including migrants; portrayal of migrants as fundamentally different and unable to integrate, thus excluding them; a call for migrants to conform to existing norms without offering them full participation in society.

When applied to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): denial of systemic issues (protections or affirmative actions are unnecessary or overreaching); security concerns, skepticism about the motives and legitimacy of asylum seekers;  discourse shifts focus from societal or structural solutions to individual responsibility; criticism of preferential treatment. Remember, compassionate criticisms might question the necessity of certain protections or the legitimacy of migrants (difference from exchange-based anti-solidarity).

Härtefälle, Herr de Maizière? Jedes Flüchtlingskind in Deutschland, das auf seine Eltern wartet, ist ein humanitärer Härtefall und nichts anderes. Die Verweigerung des Flüchtlingsnachzugs bleibt grundgesetz- und menschenrechtswidrig. Ein besonderer Skandal ist der Umgang mit kranken Flüchtlingen. Das ist eine Schande und unseres Rechtsstaates unwürdig, meine Damen und Herren. Künftig soll, ungeachtet der Gefahren für Leib und Leben, abgeschoben werden, wenn ein ärztliches Attest nicht unverzüglich vorgelegt wurde. Das ist allein objektiv an vielen Stellen schlicht unmöglich.

Explanation: The input text is an emotional appeal criticizing policies that prevent family reunification for refugee children and the treatment of sick refugees in Germany. It denotes a humanitarian perspective, emphasizing the moral and legal obligations to address the hardships faced by refugees, particularly children separated from their parents and sick individuals. The text argues that denying family reunification and deporting sick refugees without giving them a proper chance to present medical certificates is inhumane and dishonorable, and contrary to constitutional and human rights.

The overall message strongly supports the needs and rights of refugees, stating that the current handling of these cases is a disgrace. The focus on the vulnerability of refugee children and sick refugees aligns with core elements of compassionate solidarity. The text does not comment on exchange or group identity but stresses the innate human rights and the state's obligations to uphold them.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

In denen steht, was dort passiert. Dort wird ganz bewusst die Wohninfrastruktur und die Gesundheitsinfrastruktur von russischen Bombern zerstört, damit den Menschen in und aus Syrien jede Rückkehrperspektive genommen wird. Das ist nicht in Ordnung! So werden Hunderttausende von neuen Flüchtlingen erzeugt. Dafür ist Russland verantwortlich, meine sehr verehrten Damen und Herren. Wir stehen also vor wichtigen Entscheidungen, und ich finde, dass wir der Bundeskanzlerin Erfolg wünschen sollten. Wir sollten nicht die Frage stellen, was passiert, wenn sie keinen Erfolg hat.

Explanation: The given German text speaks about the deliberate destruction of living and health infrastructure by Russian bombers in Syria, which strips Syrians of the possibility to return to their homes and thus creates hundreds of thousands of new refugees. The text blames Russia for this situation and briefly mentions support for the German Chancellor in making important decisions. 

The text shows an understanding of the plight of the people in and from Syria while assigning responsibility for their situation. It implicitly calls for some action or decision to address this situation and takes a stance against the actions that have led to the creation of refugees. However, it does not explicitly express solidarity towards the migrants, nor does it convey direct support or opposition. There are no indicators of the support being conditional, a call for unity, or any mention of exchange value or shared goals with the refugees. The mention of supporting the Chancellor could imply wishing her success in dealing with the crisis, which can carry a hint of compassionate support as it is concerned with the well-being of the refugees.

Since there are no explicit expressions of solidarity or anti-solidarity, and the text is more about political commentary with only a subtle hint of compassion, it does not squarely fit into any of the subtypes of SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY. The most fitting classification would be a very subtle form of COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY due to the text’s acknowledgment of the refugees' hardships and the wrongness of creating such situations.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

Das zeigt sich insbesondere an der unerträglichen Taktiererei mit dem türkischen Ministerpräsidenten Erdogan. Jetzt soll die deutsche Polizei sogar mit der türkischen bei der Fluchtabwehr zusammenarbeiten. Skandalös! Erdogan führt einen furchtbaren Krieg gegen die eigene Bevölkerung, insbesondere gegen die Kurdinnen und Kurden, und er schiebt sogar Flüchtlinge nach Syrien ab. Wissen Sie, dass in etlichen kurdischen Gebieten seit Wochen ein 24-stündiges Ausgehverbot besteht? Das heißt, Menschen erhalten keine medizinische Versorgung, können nicht einkaufen. Teilweise ist die Wasser- und Stromversorgung unterbrochen.

Explanation: The input text strongly expresses opposition to the cooperation between German and Turkish police in the context of managing refugee movements and criticizes the actions of the Turkish government, particularly against the Kurdish population, including the forcible return of refugees to Syria and restricting the movement and access to necessities in Kurdish areas. The criticism reflects concerns about human rights violations and the well-being of the refugees and local populations affected by the actions of the Turkish government. 

The text does not directly address the groups of migrants within Germany (refugees, expellees, asylum seekers, or immigrants) or their situations. Instead, it focuses on criticizing international relations and actions that affect migrants before they might arrive in Germany. Nonetheless, the critical stance against human rights abuses and concern for the plight of people like the Kurds in Turkey shows a certain level of solidarity with those affected, which aligns with the broader concept of SOLIDARITY.

This concern for the well-being of affected populations, without mentioning specific contributions or exchange of benefits, or distinct group identities and characteristics, suggests the subtype COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. It underscores the basic human rights element of the author's stance and the call for support based exclusively on the needs and vulnerabilities of the people involved.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

Analyze the following German text and classify it into one of the high-level categories regarding migrants (refugees ― Flüchtlinge, expellees ― Vertriebene, asylum seekers ― Asylbewerber, immigrants ― Einwanderer, and other migrant categories within Germany): SOLIDARITY, ANTI-SOLIDARITY, MIXED, or NONE. If applicable, further specify by choosing the most appropriate subtype (EMPATHIC, EXCHANGE-BASED, GROUP-BASED, COMPASSIONATE) within SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Begin your response by providing the high-level category and then the subtype, if applicable.
    SOLIDARITY: Involves expressions that promote understanding, support, and unity with different groups or individuals (migrants in our case), often emphasizing shared goals, compassion, mutual assistance, and empathic understanding. Consider cases with even slight expressions of solidarity, regardless of the main topic of the text.
    ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Entails expressions that show opposition, disregard, or exclusion towards certain groups or individuals (migrants in our case). This includes emphasizing differences, denying the need for support or assistance, highlighting unequal exchanges between groups, and disregarding the unique characteristics or needs of certain groups. Even slight expressions of anti-solidarity should be considered, irrespective of the primary focus of the text.
    MIXED: A mixed stance toward migrants is characterized by the presence of both supportive and opposing expressions within the same text. This stance emerges in discussions where acknowledgment of migrants' rights, contributions, or needs is juxtaposed with limitations, conditions, or reservations that counteract or diminish the initial support. Key features of a mixed stance include (but are not limited with): conditional hospitality and selective support; balanced policies (e.g. improve the situation of migrants already within the country, while simultaneously seeking to regulate or limit further influx); expressions of empathy or concern for migrants' hardships, contrasted with discussions on practical constraints, such as societal integration challenges, or national security concerns.
    NONE: Texts which neither express solidarity nor anti-solidarity toward migrants in Germany, reflecting a neutral position or the absence of any specific stance. The absence of overt support or opposition does not automatically lead to a NONE classification; subtle cues or implicit messages may still align with solidarity or anti-solidarity categories.

    If the text falls into SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY, please specify further by choosing the most appropriate subtype from the following, after the initial high-level classification:
    
    For SOLIDARITY: EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY, GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY: Is coded when a group is different from others and this should be recognized, supported, valued. In applying empathic solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): recognition of diversity and individuality (cultural, linguistic, and personal, etc.); emphasis on the importance of preserving migrants' identities when integrating them into new communities; advocating for the right to live authentically without fear of persecution or discrimination; challenging stereotypes, prejudices against migrants; willingness to shape a diverse and dynamic community.
    EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY: Is coded when a speaker refers to the usefulness of ‘exchange partners’ in terms of their actual or future contributions (economic, cultural, or social, etc.) or willingness to contribute. In applying exchange-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): emphasis on the importance of migrants' work, skills, and cultural diversity as essential for the host society; support for migrants which is framed as an investment in individuals who contribute to the community; conditional hospitality.
    GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY: Is coded when solidarity is based on the idea of unity and support among members of a group, driven by shared characteristics, goals, interests, values and norms, or common rights and duties. The support might be driven by shared characteristics or challenges aiming at broader societal change; fostering inclusivity, equality, societal cohesion (difference from compassionate solidarity). In applying group-based solidarity to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): a unified effort to address and advocate for migrants' rights, equality, and representation; advocacy aimed at ensuring migrants' full integration; active stance against discrimination and xenophobia.
    COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY: Emphasizes providing support to marginalized, disadvantaged, or vulnerable groups, focusing on aid without expecting anything in return. It involves recognizing vulnerabilities, advocating for assistance to alleviate hardships, and offering support purely based on need. While not all indicators must be present, the core of compassionate solidarity lies in acknowledging and addressing the needs of those in vulnerable positions. The support is aimed at addressing vulnerabilities and needs without expectation of reciprocity; alleviating suffering and providing assistance based on need (difference from group-based solidarity).
    
    For ANTI-SOLIDARITY: GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Definitions:
    EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Refers to the cases when a speaker refers to a group indicating that this group is different from everyone else, but this difference should not be respected or recognised. This can be expressed in: an overt or covert refusal to acknowledge the validity of a group's diverse identities, roles, and choices. Opposition to the group's efforts to define their identity, roles, and aspirations on their own terms. Promotion of a return to or maintenance of traditional societal roles and structures that restrict the group's opportunities.
    EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Focuses on the idea that some groups take more than they give. It brings attention to groups that seem to receive a lot of resources or support, but do not contribute much or cause problems. This perspective supports punishing such groups, giving them less help, or making them contribute more. This can be expressed in suggesting that this group should be punished, or get less than they are currently getting; calling for the group to give or do more in return for help. Remember: exchange-based criticisms focus on the economic implications and perceived unfairness in resource distribution (difference from compassionate anti-solidarity).
    GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY: Points to an out-group characterised by pronounced differences, and an expression of exclusion. This can be expressed in: a stronger emphasis on one's own group's interests (e.g., We must stand up for our own interests...); resistance to support of, assimilation or integration with other groups. With migrants, this can be expressed as an emphasis on protecting interests of the native population at the expense of including migrants; portrayal of migrants as fundamentally different and unable to integrate, thus excluding them; a call for migrants to conform to existing norms without offering them full participation in society.
    COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY: It is about disregarding and excluding individuals or subgroups, refusing to support them either on the basis that they are perceived to be in a good position already, or are viewed as undeserving of help. When applied to migrants, this can be expressed by (but not limited with): denial of systemic issues (protections or affirmative actions are unnecessary or overreaching); security concerns, skepticism about the motives and legitimacy of asylum seekers;  discourse shifts focus from societal or structural solutions to individual responsibility; criticism of preferential treatment. Remember, compassionate criticisms might question the necessity of certain protections or the legitimacy of migrants (difference from exchange-based anti-solidarity).
    
    Think step by step. Begin by analyzing the text to identify its high-level category related to migrants: SOLIDARITY, ANTI-SOLIDARITY, MIXED, or NONE. If the text falls under SOLIDARITY or ANTI-SOLIDARITY, further specify by identifying the most appropriate subtype: EMPATHIC, EXCHANGE-BASED, GROUP-BASED, or COMPASSIONATE. After your comprehensive analysis, conclude with 'Label:' followed by the most fitting category name. If identifying a subtype, combine it with the high-level category, such as GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY, GROUP-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EXCHANGE-BASED ANTI-SOLIDARITY, EMPATHIC ANTI-SOLIDARITY, COMPASSIONATE ANTI-SOLIDARITY. Please choose just one label.

Ueber die Ursache dieser Erscheinung, über die ökonomischen Wirkungen derselben will ich mich hier keineswegs aussprechen; iH weise aber darauf hin, daß demzufolge die Streitigkeiten dieser ausländischen Arbeiter mit ihren Dienstherren sich um so mehr vermehren werden, je größer die Zahl derselben wird. Wenn nun aber Deutschland diese fremden Arbeitskräfte thatsächlich beschäftigt, so erscheint es nach meinem Dafürhalten als ein nobile oillcium des Reichs, die Ansprüche wegen des in Deutschland erdienteu Arbeitslohns diesen ausländischen Arbeitern in einer möglichen Art und Weise zu garantiren und ihnen die Rechtsverfolgung in Deutschland möglichst zu erleichtern. Wenn wir die Kräfte dieser Arbeiter in Anspruch nehmen, dürfte auch der Gesetzgeber verpflichtet sein, für Erreichung der RechtsVerfolgung einzutreten. Nun meine ich, daß unsere Zivilprozeßordnung nach zweifacher Richtung hin Beschränkungen kennt, welche für die Verfolgung von Lohnansprüchen seitens der Ausländer als eine ganz besonders drückende Last empfunden werden müssen. So weise ich zunächst auf Z 102 der Zivilprozeßordnung hin, der besagt: Ausländer, welche als Kläger auftreten, haben dem Beklagten auf dessen Verlangen für die Prozeßkosten Sicherheit zu leisten. Diese Verpflichtung trifft nur in fünf Fällen nicht ein: einmal, wenn nach den Gesetzen des Staats, welchem der Kläger angehört, ein Deutscher in gleichem Falle zur Sicherheitsleistung nicht verpflichtet ist. Ich meine, daß man das Verlangen einer Kautionsleistung für einen ausländischen Arbeiter, der seinen erdienten Lohn einklagt, als ein durchaus unbegründetes ansehen muß; auf diese Weise wird ein solcher Arbeiter, der doch keine Geldmittel hat, um große Kautionen zu stellen, schutzund rechtlos, denn wenn er die Kaution nicht bestellt, wird seine Klage kostenpflichtig abgewiesen.

Explanation: The provided German text discusses the challenges faced by foreign workers (ausländische Arbeiter) in Germany regarding the pursuit of their wage claims in the German legal system. The author expresses a clear position that the state should facilitate these foreign workers' access to legal recourse and guarantee their rights to wages earned in Germany. The author criticizes the requirement for foreign plaintiffs to provide security for legal costs, which is seen as unfair and burdensome, particularly for those without the financial means to meet such requirements.

This text displays a stance of SOLIDARITY with foreign workers, advocating for the protection of their legal rights and the alleviation of the specific hardships they face. The text does not directly discuss the broader categories of migrants such as refugees, expellees, asylum seekers, and immigrants, but it does focus on the subset of foreign workers within the broader context of migration.

To classify the subtype within SOLIDARITY:

- The text does not explicitly refer to an emotional connection with the foreign workers or a recognition of their diversity (therefore, not EMPATHIC SOLIDARITY).
- Although it involves foreign workers, the text does not primarily emphasize the workers' contributions or exchanges with the host society (thus, not EXCHANGE-BASED SOLIDARITY).
- The advocacy does not center around a call for unity among a group based on shared characteristics, nor is it explicitly about fostering societal cohesion or battling discrimination (so, not GROUP-BASED SOLIDARITY).
- The argument is situated in addressing a vulnerability (the inability to secure legal representation and claim earned wages) and offering support without an expectation of reciprocity. The author is advocating for assistance to alleviate the specific hardship of access to the legal system (hence, COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY is the most fitting subtype).

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

Ein anderes Beispiel. Die Mittel für den sozialen Wohnungsbau werden erhöht. Das ist eine richtige Maßnahme und auch ein wichtiges Thema mit Blick auf die Akzeptanz in der Gesellschaft. Wir schaffen Wohnraum für Flüchtlinge, aber auch für die Menschen in der Gesellschaft, die Unterstützung brauchen. Sie stellen dafür zusätzlich 500 Millionen Euro ein. Damit kommen wir auf einen Betrag von 1 Milliarde Euro. Wir wissen aber: Wir brauchen im kommunalen Wohnungsbau ein Programm, das 2 Milliarden Euro umfasst.

Explanation: Analyzing the provided German text, it discusses the increase in funding for social housing construction which is seen as a positive measure and an important issue for societal acceptance. It emphasizes the creation of living space for refugees but also for other people in society who need support and mentions a specific financial commitment to this cause. The text suggests a need for an even larger program to meet needs in municipal housing. 

This text can be categorized under SOLIDARITY because it speaks about measures taken to accommodate both refugees and other members of society in need, without any negative connotation or opposition towards these groups. The focus on increased funding for social housing construction aimed at helping refugees among others, without expecting anything in return from them, can be further classified as COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. The support seems to be based on their need for housing without direct mention of any expected contribution from them in return.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY

Die Hürden für Asyl sind sehr hoch, aber in Abschiebehaft geht man ganz schnell. Auch bei der Einstufung von Abschiebeterminen als Dienstgeheimnis gab es eine Korrektur. Amtspersonen müssen dann künftig mit einer Haftstrafe von fünf Jahren rechnen, Ähnliches gilt auch für andere Personen. Damit kriminalisieren Sie die Solidarität mit Flüchtlingen. Meine Damen und Herren, ein anderer Punkt ist das verfassungswidrige Aushungern von anerkannten Schutzflüchtlingen, auch von Familien mit Kindern, die aus einem anderen EU-Staat nach Deutschland kommen, zum Beispiel aus Griechenland, Bulgarien oder Italien, wo Flüchtlinge schutzlos auf der Straße landen. Sie bekommen hier keine Sozialhilfe mehr. Ohne jede inhaltliche Begründung werden nach dem Willen der Koalition die gutachterlichen Stellungnahmen von Psychotherapeuten nicht mehr als Abschiebehindernis akzeptiert.

Explanation: The provided German text points out the difficulties facing asylum seekers and refugees in Germany, touching upon the high barriers to asylum, the swift process towards detention for deportation, the penalisation of solidarity actions with refugees, and the unconstitutional practices of denying social aid to recognized refugees from other EU countries, including families with children. The text also criticizes the practice of not accepting expert opinions from psychotherapists as an obstacle to deportation.

Based on this analysis, the text falls into the SOLIDARITY category, as it expresses support and concern for migrants' rights and wellbeing, emphasizing the hardships and injustices they face.

To further specify within the subtypes of SOLIDARITY, the text most aligns with COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY. This is because it emphasizes the need to provide support to vulnerable groups (i.e., the refugees and asylum seekers mentioned), it acknowledges the vulnerability of these individuals, advocates for assistance to alleviate their hardships, and it criticizes policies that deny such support without looking for any benefit in return.

 Label: COMPASSIONATE SOLIDARITY
